Hello,

Thank you for the suggestion. As there are many options that one could potentially want to pass in to checkM, the current way that I'm handling this is by giving the user the option to run checkM on their own (with whatever options they want, including `-reduced_tree`), and then pass the results of that run on to dRep.

This is currently possible using the `--Chdb` option. To generate the table needed for this input, the two checkM commands are:

```
checkm taxonomy_wf domain Bacteria ${prodigal_output} checkM_outdir/ -f checkM_outdir/results.tsv --tab_table -g -x faa

checkm qa checkM_outdir/Bacteria.ms checkM_outdir/ -f checkM_outdir/Chdb.tsv --tab_table -o 2
```

Of course, if you're confident that you're genome are complete, you could also skip checkM entirely.

Let me know if you have any trouble!
-Matt

However the problem I've been getting at all along is that doing ``eventlet.monkey_patch()`` before importing tenacity doesn't solve the issue.

Cinder already does this early on (before importing tenacity) in it's UTs and ``time.sleep`` cannot subsequently be patched as you'd expect in UTs: https://github.com/openstack/cinder/blob/master/cinder/tests/unit/__init__.py#L30

The way I got this to work is if you change ``cinder.unit.tests.__init__`` to something like:

```py
import time

import eventlet
import mock
from six.moves import builtins

from cinder import objects

eventlet.monkey_patch()
# patch up time sleep before tenacity is imported
mocked_retry_sleep = mock.patch.object(time, 'sleep')
# now consumers can access mocked_retry_sleep if needed
```

So best I can tell, ``time.sleep`` needs to be mock patched before tenacity is imported. This behavior is the reason I opened this issue.

The PyP developers have put a lot of effort into improving ``cpyext`` recently, and it's much better now. Many extension modules compile and run out of the box now. The emulation is substantially slower than the native C-API in CPython, sure, but that only applies to the boundary operations. In the case of Lupa, if you pass data into Lua, process it there for a while, and then pass the result back, that can easily be as fast in PyPy as in CPython. If, OTOH, you create an iterator in Lua and run over it in Python, then that usage will suffer from the emulation layer overhead.

There's a separate but related bug here: l = line.split(None, 1)

The HTTP RFC indicates the delim character between headers and values is ":".  dpkt current and this patch assume there's a space following the header name.

i.e., 
Header:Value   
vs. 
Header: Value

Both are valid HTTP headers.  That block is more appropriately:

 15         line = line.strip()

@crass That's not boolean algebra, but rather multiplication with more than one bits per factor and bitwise AND. Consider this:

    In [1]: True and False
    Out[1]: False

    In [2]: True * False
    Out[2]: 0

    In [3]: True and True
    Out[3]: True

    In [4]: True * True
    Out[4]: 1

When regular multiplication is used strictly with one bit per factor it is the same as boolean AND.
And just to be clear:

    In [5]: True == 1
    Out[5]: True

    In [6]: False == 0
    Out[6]: True

`daff` currently has a tough time with repeated rows, its algorithm does best when it can cobble together some kind of approximate primary key for the table. That said, it should have been able to do better than this. I've added a commit that fixes an asymmetry in a relevant heuristic (`daff diff b a` was not bad) and gives a better result for your case.

For tuning, the main control doesn't apply in your case - if you happen to know which set of columns are a good choice as a primary key, you can tell daff that with `--id col1 --id col2 --id col3`. But your case doesn't really have a key.

You can shorten the diff a bit with `--context 0` just to show only rows with changes.

In bgapi.py add time.sleep(2) after the device and serial port reset. I'm on OSX High Sierra and this works for me. Modified code below:

`    def start(self):
        """
        Connect to the USB adapter, reset it's state and start a backgroud
        receiver thread.
        """
        if self._running and self._running.is_set():
            self.stop()

        # Fail immediately if no device is attached, don't retry waiting for one
        # to be plugged in.
        self._open_serial_port(max_connection_attempts=1)

        log.info("Resetting and reconnecting to device for a clean environment")
        # Blow everything away and start anew.
        # Only way to be sure is to burn it down and start again.
        # (Aka reset remote state machine)
        # Note: Could make this a conditional based on parameter if this
        # happens to be too slow on some systems.

        # The zero param just means we want to do a normal restart instead of
        # starting a firmware update restart.

        self.send_command(CommandBuilder.system_reset(0))
        self._ser.flush()
        self._ser.close()

        time.sleep(2)

        self._open_serial_port()
        self._receiver = threading.Thread(target=self._receive)
        self._receiver.daemon = True

        self._running = threading.Event()
        self._running.set()
        self._receiver.start()
`

Ah, okay.  The problem is in my logic for trying to detect whether a command-line argument was explicitly set.  That `if` statement checking to see if the command-line argument value is *different* than the default value with the assumption that that will conclusively tell us whether or not the command-line argument was actually specified by the user.

In other words, if we ran

    green

Then `termcolor` would be `None` AND the argument was NOT specified on the command-line.  In this case, the logic "seems" to work, but it's just by chance.  The command really wasn't specified on the command-line, therefore checking to see if the value from argparse was the same as the default seemed to validate that it wasn't supplied on the command-line.

But it's a lie!  Because:

    green -t

Provides the exact same conditions.  But the logic that is supposed to detect whether the value came via command line falsely claims it didn't, since the value was actually provided, it just happened to be the same value as it would have been (via argparse) in the first place.

Anyway, I'm not sure if I'm making sense, but I'm aware of the actual problem and I'm trying to figure out a sane way to fix it.

For the Curve 14-Curve 15 (ID: 11) case, there are 12 candidates and two of them cause `from_linearized()` to fail:

![figure_3](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/520669/36181402-8fee87c6-10d9-11e8-94ef-66faa921c976.png)

The two "in the middle" (i.e. not on an edge) both cause `segment_intersection()` to fail, which in turn causes a `NotImplementedError: Line segments parallel.`.

That's because, in both cases, the "linearized" versions of the curves are parallel.

It's worth noting, these were "detected" by approximating each surface by a polygon (by just evaluating at 64 points along each edge, i.e. `0, 1/64, ..., 63/64`) and then intersecting the polygons using [`Shapely`][1].

I tried to be careful to figure out what type of intersection there was (i.e. `Polygon` vs. `MultiPolygon` vs. `LineString` etc.) but I'm fairly certain that accessing `.area` will work in a consistent way:

```python
s_vals = np.linspace(0, 1, 65)[:65]
params = np.empty((192, 2), order='F')
# First edge is (s, 0)
params[:64, 0] = s_vals
params[:64, 1] = 0.0
# Second edge is (1 - s, s)
params[64:128, 0] = 1.0 - s_vals
params[64:128, 1] = s_vals
# Third edge is (0, 1 - s)
params[128:, 0] = 0.0
params[128:, 1] = 1.0 - s_vals

evaluated1 = surface1.evaluate_cartesian_multi(params)
polygon1 = shapely.geometry.Polygon(evaluated1.T)

evaluated1 = surface2.evaluate_cartesian_multi(params)
polygon2 = shapely.geometry.Polygon(polygon2.T)

intersection = polygon1.intersection(polygon2)
print(intersection.area)
```

[1]: https://pypi.org/project/Shapely/1.6.4.post1/

>  It looks like if I load a MIB once, pySNMP stores it permanently. Is this correct?

The pysnmp library asks `pysmi` library to compile original ASN.1 MIBs into a set of Python classes and caches these Pythonized MIBs in [`~/.pysnmp/mibs/`](https://github.com/etingof/pysnmp/blob/master/pysnmp/smi/compiler.py#L12) (on UNIX). This phase only runs if the caller is asking for a MIB which is not yet cached. You can remove this cache at any moment by hand.

>  I originally had to perform a getCmd to get the name of an agent where my varBinds parameter was ....

Yes, that's MIB cache at work.

Yes, and you can change the default cache directory by passing `destination='/your/dir` kwargs to [.addAsn1MibSource](http://pysnmp.sourceforge.net/docs/api-reference.html#pysnmp.smi.rfc1902.ObjectIdentity.addAsn1MibSource).

Your strategy is doing the following:

1. You don't have a Run*(RunDaily,RunMonthly, etc.) algo to start with so it runs on every datetime in the dataframe.
2. bt.algos.SelectAll() then saves all of the securities with prices above 0 to target.temp['selected']
3. bt.algos.WeighTarget(tw_P) then saves the weights not with NA values in target.temp['weights'] = w.dropna(). Note this doesn't use anything from SelectAll().
4. bt.algos.WeighInvVol(lookback=pd.DateOffset(months=3),lag=pd.DateOffset(days=0)) then takes the securities from target.temp['selected'](set by SelectAll()) and overwrites target.temp['weights'] to have their weights have equal volatility.
5. bt.algos.LimitWeights(limit=0.7) then goes through target.temp['weights'] and takes any weight > 70% and redistributes it proportionally to the other securities in target.temp['weights']
6. bt.algos.Rebalance() then takes the weights in target.temp['weights'] and transacts to change the portfolio to match the desired weights.

In summary, this error is cause because you have both bt.algos.WeighTarget(tw_P) and bt.algos.WeighInvVol(lookback=pd.DateOffset(months=3),lag=pd.DateOffset(days=0)) running.

Behind the scenes, the base requests (`asks.get/post/etc`) all create a `Session` object, use it, and dump it. I was relying on python to GC the sockets, but it turns out we can create sockets much much faster than they are cleaned up, in most cases.

I'll be implementing some context-manager like behaviour on the `Session`, which I'll use in the base requests. May as well make that available for use generally too. I think it could be very nice :)

Example

```python
async def example(url_list):
    with asks.Session(connections=10) as s:
        for url in url_list:
            print(await s.get(url))
```

In general, though, there is no problem in doing this. Any matrix/function that includes \delta t needs to be recomputed before the update/predict cycles so the computations are correct, but otherwise everything proceeds apace. For example, the state transition matrix F contains \delta t for motion models. If measurements come in at time t=0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, then \delta t = 0.1 for the first 2 predict calls, and then \delta t = 0.2 for the third (to account for gap between 0.3 to 0.5.

If you mean *out of sequence* measurements, e.g. t = 3, t = 5, t = 4, then no. There is theory to account for this, but I have not studied it, and thus not implemented it. This library was created for pedagogical reasons, so there are some real limits to the library.

I ended to do something else:

    ## Brief text here
    ## @namespace package_name.module_name

While `doxypypy` generates something like this instead:

    ## @brief  Brief text here

To the OP: using `@package` is not good, since it is cumulative and applies to all modules of a package. A Python module is treated as a namespace for Doxygen, at least if you choose `OPTIMIZE_OUTPUT_JAVA`, which seems recommended for Python.

@10000shiro  Cloud you propose the update of dictionary or a better way for processing? 

>The 〇 in the snippet together with the 三〇 was meant to translate to 30.

In fact, `三` is registered as 'み' or other pronounce in kakasidict. Internally a way as same as original KAKASI, `三` is converted to  'み' and then  'み' converted to 'Mi' in `J2a` mode. Because `J2a` mode is realized with `J2H` (Japanese to Hiragana, by lookup of dictionary) and `H2a` (Hiragana to romaji) conversion.

You can register '〇' as '０' in a translation table in pykakasi/sym2.py but '三〇' may be converted to  'Mi0'. You also can register '三〇' as '３０’ in Kana-Kanji dictioanry, then it cloud be converted to '30'. 
So how to deal  '三〇〇'、  '二〇〇' 、 '三〇二〇', ... and infinite combinations of numbers?

You can easily observe dictionary using `grep`  command such as 'grep 三 pykakasi/data/kakasidict.utf8`

I think increasing the buffer size is not an option, because the buffer you
need to increase is on the producer side, and is not controllable by the
consumer.

Some form of threading is needed somewhere. Either it's done by
subprocess.communicate (imposing the restriction that you can't get output
in real-time) or by pup.

Although I suppose you could do reads with timeouts, and if you get a
timeout you could read as much as possible from stderr (with timeout)
before going back to reading stdin.

Option 2 opens you to the possibility that someone deadlocks on git fetch.

Closing the stderr stream is also a possibility, but of course you don't
get the data.

On Mar 2, 2017 6:29 AM, "Markus Siemens" <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> So I've thought a little about how we can solve this. It appears to me we
> have the following options:
>
>    - Increase the buffer size. This is only a band-aid really, as we
>    don't know beforehand how much stderr output there will be.
>    - The solution you proposed seems to just wait for the subprocess to
>    finish and then process stdout and stderr. This avoids the buffer
>    problem but it also means we print the contents stdout after the
>    subprocess finished instead of printing it live. Right now, we only need
>    stdout printing for git fetch. So we could probably have a special
>    implementation that does live output the way we do it now. This
>    implementation then would only be used for git fetch where we probably
>    won't run into buffering problems.
>    - A more general solution would be to move stderr processing off to a
>    separate thread (using something like this:
>    http://stackoverflow.com/a/31867499/997063
>    <http://stackoverflow.com/a/31867499/997063>). This would be the most
>    clean solution but would introduce multithreading as a potential source of
>    problems. And I guess it may be just plain overkill.

The solution here is to pass in `api_version=#` when creating your KafkaConsumer or KafkaProducer. If we enabled configuring a timeout to check_version, it would still just raise an exception and crash and that isn't ideal (yes, the crash could be faster). The reason for the crash is that the client must have some value for api_version to operate.

@jeffwidman As a starting point it would be good to always log `offset` of the produced message after the future is resolved. If the owner claims they wait for futures, it should be possible. If the offset was returned by Broker, but was not found by Consumer (or that offset contains another message) it's likely a Broker issue...

`Producer.flush()` is only a signal to finish the batch and send them to Broker, it does not confirm delivery. In case of a network error or a timeout, you have to check the future to get the error. The network error can lead to a duplicate, but should not lead to a loss of the message.

The `SimpleConsumer` stack isn't actively supported by the library's primary author, as it's been deprecated since 1.0. Can you switch to using `KafkaConsumer`?

And OffsetOutOfRangeError simply means you've got a zookeeper offset that is outside the range of offsets currently on the broker. It's generally not a problem with the library, just a consumer that isn't keeping up with the broker produce rate/log rolling rate.

Kafka-python client manages group information in the same way Java client would. The deal here is that Kafka 0.8 brokers don't have the API that KafkaMonitor uses to list groups. If you need support for 0.8.2 brokers you could post an issue on KafkaMonitor side...

Your proposal would require each cell to have a unique jackknife region.  Which really means that each region would need to generate their trees separately, so you'd have `nregion` trees.  This in turn implies that you might as well do this in python, rather than in C++, since there isn't really any advantage to having the C++ layer know about the regions.

For that reason, my intention on this issue was to implement all this in Python, not C++, more or less as you described.  So I don't think what you are doing is "grossly inefficient" in that I don't think you are doing any extra calculations that could be saved if TreeCorr were smarter.

The main inefficiency is that users currently need to implement all that themselves, so there is user inefficiency in that, which I would like to fix by incorporating that algorithm into TreeCorr natively.  If you'd like to take a stab at adding that feature using the code you are currently using in the WZ taskforce, feel free to send a pull request.  :)  

Or if you send me the algorithm you are using, that would also be helpful, since it would probably save me some debugging time.

Note: One thing that I'd like to enable when implementing this feature is a keyword to select between galaxy jackknife and pair jackknife.  cf [Oliver Friedrich's analysis](https://cdcvs.fnal.gov/redmine/projects/deswlwg/wiki/50deg%5E2_patch_covariance) about the difference between the two.

Also, I'd planned to start with Chris's proposal of giving region numbers (or ids) in the catalog, but I'd probably also want to add an option to automatically generate regions using kmeans or somesuch.

@avelis Don't worry, I perfectly understand your point. I'll try to bring as more details as possible.

**1. In case of thread interruption**
If the thread is exited before the `finalize_response` method is called, well yes, we'll indeed lose the record of this request. In the current version, we would have had a record in the DB in a "dangling" state without `status_code` and `response`. In my opinion, not a big deal if we lose this kind of records.

**2. Prevent mixin from ruining the API**
We still do have the `hasattr` tests both in `handle_exception` ([#L85](https://github.com/aschn/drf-tracking/blob/master/rest_framework_tracking/mixins.py#L85)) and `finalize_response` ([#L100](https://github.com/aschn/drf-tracking/blob/master/rest_framework_tracking/mixins.py#L100)). So we are quite assured that we do have a log in our request before doing anything.

What could happen indeed is a problem during the final `save()`. To be fully safe, we could wrap the call in a `try: except: pass`.

However, in the current version, if something fails during the initial object creation, I'm not sure the API continues gently. As we see on [line #57](https://github.com/aschn/drf-tracking/blob/master/rest_framework_tracking/mixins.py#L57), the `try` is caught with a `return` statement, meaning that the method will stop here and the parent `initial` method not called ; which will probably break the rest of the request pipeline.
If we finally decide to not merge this PR, maybe we should make a fix on this, replacing `return`by `pass`. 

To address these two last points, we surely could make a test, mocking model `create()`/`save()` calls to raise an exception and check that the status code of the response is still 200.

That's it! I hope I answered all your questions :)

I like the idea of using only one parameter instead of two, that's what we already have. For backwards compatibility it is easier to keep `url_geo` as someone could already define a custom URL for it.

I don't like supporting two formats of the URL that return either IP in plaintext or JSON, as this is extra code (extra code is more complicated to read) and could lead to confusion (users might provide URL that returns IP in plaintext, but then specify a format that contains `{ip}` and `{country}`, and be confused that it doesn't work). 
 
Historically it made sense to use URL that returns IP and nothing more, but since then the module grew, it now can do so much more, I think it is worth cleaning the code in favor of the new functionality. My respect to the author doesn't reduce by not using their service to determine IP address 😉

With this change, we deprecate the plaintext format and unite all users, and if anyone defined `url` this param is now just ignored and everyone is using the URL that returns JSON response.

I wanted to share that I like `env(EXAMPLE)` more than `$EXAMPLE`.
1) because it appears to be more pythonic... like how python interpreters uses `exit()` to exit.. and shells uses `exit`.
2) this could make noises on python syntax.
3) seeing `env()` would be more obvious telling that it is using `environment variables` than just `$` and maybe feels more clean too.

```python
# PYTHON
order += "my_module"
order += env(ORDER_MODULE)

module {
    normal_parameter = 'some value'
    env_parameter = env(SOME_ENVIRONMENT_PARAM)
    sensitive_api_key = env(API_KEY)

    complex_parameter = {
      'key': env(VAL)
    }

    list_of_tuples = [
      (env(APPLE_NUM, int), 'apple'),
      (2, env(ORANGE))
    ]

    float_param = env(MY_NUM, float)
}
```
```python
# SHELL
order += "my_module"
order += $ORDER_MODULE

module {
    normal_parameter = 'some value'
    env_parameter = $SOME_ENVIRONMENT_PARAM
    sensitive_api_key = $API_KEY

    complex_parameter = {
      'key': $VAL
    }

    list_of_tuples = [
      ($APPLE_NUM, int), 'apple'),  # probably wrong, auto-guessing.
      (2, $ORANGE)
    ]
    float_param = $MY_NUM, float    # probably wrong, auto-guessing.
}
```

This ridiculously large Pull Requests actually includes three merges, but with good reason. Initially, this PR only included dynamic themeing, but given how the coverage on this feature was very low because it could not be tested in the Tk 8.5 environments that the CI provides, I decided to immediately also incorporate a way to load PNG-based themes on Tk 8.5 environments (thus making it possible to load theme `Arc` on Python 2.7). This turned into a distribution nightmare when I concluded that this would require building wheels instead of source distributions if I didn't want users to have to install the TkImg library for all platforms on their systems. Thus I also had to adjust the CI settings to automatically build wheels (as I can only build `manylinux` `64-bit` wheels myself). Thus, now the following wheels should be provided upon a new tag release:
- `manylinux` wheels for Python 2.7, 3.4, 3.5 and 3.6
- `darwin` wheels for Python 2.7 and 3.6
- `windows` wheels for Python 2.7, 3.4, 3.5 and 3.6

Thus, in short: dynamic themeing required TkImg for coverage which depended on wheels for proper installation. Now, do the wheels work? Only one way to find out: Tag a new release on master and test the wheels that get generated in a VM (except for the `darwin` ones).

Of course, on 32-bit platforms and other systems I cannot make wheels for, it is possible to use `python setup.py install` still. Though, if you need BSD support or something more exotic, then you will have to compile TkImg yourself.

Yes, I understand, but unfortunately it's not easy to accommodate that use case. To understand why, you need to know that bash completion works by keying on the first word on the command line (`python` in this case) and dispatching to a shell function registered as its completer, followed by a global fallback completer if that function returns no completions. Take a look at https://github.com/kislyuk/argcomplete/blob/master/scripts/register-python-argcomplete and https://github.com/kislyuk/argcomplete/blob/master/argcomplete/bash_completion.d/python-argcomplete.sh to understand how those shell functions work. One of the two functions would have to understand that you're trying to run a module that supports completion - either by overloading the completer function for `python` or, in the global fallback case, by finding the module separately and scanning its contents for the global completion magic string - and then run the module in completion mode.

You're welcome to try doing this, but I'm afraid any solution here may be brittle.

Hello Julien,

I've looked for a good solution for handling signals in the time domain. 

Pandas series only works if all your channels have the same timebase, and most of the times you will have channels acquired at different rates from different sources.

Please consider this scenario:
``` python
from pandas import Series
from asammdf import Signal
import numpy
numpy.set_printoptions(threshold=numpy.nan)

df1 = {'t': [0.1*x for x in range(10)],
       'vals' : [3 for x in range(10)]}

df2 = {'t': [0.1*x + 0.05 for x in range(10)],
       'vals' : [3 for x in range(10)]}

S1 = Series(df1['vals'], df1['t'])
S2 = Series(df2['vals'], df2['t'])

print("Pandas Series add", S1.add(S2), sep='\n')

S1 = Signal(df1['vals'], df1['t'], name='s1')
S2 = Signal(df2['vals'], df2['t'], name='s2')

print('\n')

print("asammdf Signal add", S1 + S2, sep='\n')
```

Actually, when an external (unknown) process modifies the monitored file, there should be an event, but if the script itself modifies a file, there shouldn't be an event.
The actual goal is to update mtime with ctime for all files that get modified by an external event because setfacl / chmod only updates ctime and rsync uses mtime to determine if the file is newer.
I've eventually managed to write something that does handle my problem, it's just not beautiful, but works:

```
	def on_modified(self, event):
		print(event.event_type)
		print(event.key)
		if (event.src_path == self.replica.path):
			return
		# Ignore event if self modification
		if (event.src_path in self.ignoreevents):
			self.ignoreevents.remove(event.src_path)
			return

		# Fix for Rsync: update mtime with ctime so rsync will aknowldge attr changes (chmod / setfacl only change ctime)
		if (CONFIG['RSYNC_OPTIONS']['sync_attrs'].lower() == "yes"):
			# Precision of ctime only works in Python 3+
			ctime = os.path.getctime(event.src_path)
			os.utime(event.src_path, (ctime, ctime))
			self.ignoreevents.append(event.src_path)
```

Another way, that can also keep backwards compat a bit is to (ab)use slices for defaults as callables, eg:

```python
class Record(fields.Tuple.Username[""].Created[:datetime.now].Revision[None].Active[False):
    pass
```
or even with strings for more possibilities (the init function is made from a string, alike namedtuple, so this is possible):
```python
class Record(fields.Tuple.Username[:"getpass.getuser()"].Created[:"datetime.now()"].Revision[None].Active["Revision!=None"):
    pass
```

The zappa documentation is all you need to do but perhaps I can put it in context a bit. 

With zappa you deploy your flask-ask app to aws. It's fronted by the aws api gateway which proxies all requests to aws lambda.  This is needed because flask ask is an wgsi application and Alexa service doesn't speak that. So it needs a layer. zappa sets that all up for you. :) 

In order for lambda to run your code, it needs to be complete with dependencies and libraries. Zappa packages your code on deployment and uploads it all. For this reason all code /libs etc,  including zappa itself, needs to be installed in a python virtualenv with the app. A virtualenv is an isolated environment that ensures that the sw in it can only use that set of files in it and no system resources. 

0. your base system needs to have python, pip and virtualenv (Google on python virtualenv) 
1.switch to your flask-ask app folder. 
2.create a new virtualenv there
3.activate the virtualenv 
4.install flask-ask into this virtualenv with ' pip install flask-ask'
5.install zappa into this virtualenv with ' pip install zappa'
6. Configure your aws credentials on your environment (Google 'setup awssdk credentials'). Use an admin key pair at first,  create it in aws I'm service. 
7.run zappa init 
8.run zappa deploy
9.it should end without errors with an api gw URL that you need to use in your Alexa skill https configuration. 
--
Every time you do 'zappa update', you need to be in the activated virtualenv,  else zappa can't bundle up all files and links for lambda to run it. I just created a bash script so I never forget. 

Hope this helps.

 I think we may still need to put the pack/unpack into a separate thread, since I think `tar.extractall()` will do a blocking read of the file-like socket wrapper, and that won't work when the Twisted reactor is in the driver's seat (it's a "don't call us, we'll call you" sort of arrangement). Same thing for the `tar.add()`: that will do a blocking write, and when the write buffer is full, it will just halt everything.

import BAC0
import time
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

class plot:
    @staticmethod
    def readWriteStep(device):
        device['TempA'] = 30
        device['CoilA'] = 30
        
        for i in range(30):
            if i == 20:
                device['TempA'] = 50
                device['CoilA'] = 90
            else:
                # just read it so it will be recorded
                device['TempA']
                device['CoilA']
            time.sleep(1)
            # Note here that thread nature of BAC0 and the speed of the BACnet network 
            # will maybe create imperfect 1 seconds interval readings
        
        plt.figure(1)
        plt.subplot(211)
        plt.plot(device['nciPIDHumTI'].history)
        plt.subplot(212)
        plt.plot(device['nciPIDDeshumTI'].history)
        plt.show()
        device['TempA'] = 'auto' 
        # release the override of the input as it is not typical to write to the presentValue of a analogInput
        print(device['TempA'].history)
        # Just for fun, print the history

class Application():
    @staticmethod
    def main():
        device_address = '139.6.154.83'
        device_instance = 7000 # presuming device instance is 7000.... pick yours
        object_list = [('analogInput',6015), ('analogOutput',7006)]
        bacnet = BAC0.connect() # presuming 255.255.255.0 mask
        device = BAC0.device(device_address,device_instance,bacnet, object_list = object_list, poll=0)
        # Here I will presume I know the point names... let say AI:6014 is called TempA
        # and AO:7006 is named CoilA
        # poll=0 will disable poll... the value will be saved when we read or write them

        plot.readWriteStep(device)
        bacnet.disconnect()
```

This is probably not a good idea to read a lot of points every seconds.... and there could be better ways to do that... just wanted to illustrate how I would use BAC0 features to do what you want

Another way would be using the embedded Web Server / Bokeh Feature and use
```
device['TempA'].chart()
device['CoilA'].chart()
```
And check the live trends on your browser

Thanks for your interest in BAC0 !

From mypy point of view, I think (1) and (3) are almost equally easy/hard to implement. Also the fact that we will have two complex functions with the same API seems suboptimal to me:
```python
def field(name=None, type=None, *, default=_MISSING,
          repr=True, hash=None, init=True, cmp=True): ...
# and
def field_with_default_factory(name=None, type=None, *, default_factory=None,
                               repr=True, hash=None, init=True, cmp=True): ...
```
I think we should go with the first version:
```python
@dataclass
class Foo:
    x: list = field(factory=list, repr=False)
```
Also I think that ``name`` and ``type`` arguments should not appear in _function_ field. They are of course will be present in the _class_ ``Field``, but those two will be filled in automatically using ``__dict__`` and ``__annotations__``.

Example using current version of `dataclasses`:
```python
@dataclass
class B:
    x:str = "1"
    y:int = field(default=2)
    z:int = 3
    
help(B.__init__)
Help on function __init__:

    B.__init__(self, x, y, z)

The trivial implementation, extending the dictionary of elements to contain symbols and names, would break the table iterator.  Conceptually, having more keys "in" the dictionary then are returned by iterating through it is unexpected (and therefore bad).

The type signature would be ugly, which is generally a bad sign.

The interface already provides table.Fe, table.symbol('Fe') and getattr(table, 'Fe'),  so table['Fe'] is even more redundant.  This breaks the Zen of Python:  *There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it.*

So, how compelling are the arguments to add it?

The aim of *django-machina* is to provide a solid core of a forum project that can be easily integrated into existing Django projects. Thus *django-machina* don't provide functionalities such as direct messages. It's true that often you will find standalone forum software (such as SMF) providing DM functionalities. However *django-machina* is not a standalone forum application. This kind of features is not directly related to forum applications and can be used by a wide range of applications. This is why *django-machina* does not provide direct messages or private messages : you can add a messaging system to your Django application without relying on *django-machina* (you can see https://djangopackages.org/grids/g/messaging/ for a list of applications handling private messages between users).

Hi @timrc, thanks for the contribution!

Ratelimit [explicitly does not support X-Forwarded-For](https://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/security.html#client-ip-address) in the library for the same reason Django dropped the `SetRemoteAddressFromForwardedFor` middleware: it's inappropriate to make assumptions about the way the application is set up in production. X-Forwarded-For parsing is nuanced and can open up security holes if done wrong, or even done correctly but behind proxies that don't use it (e.g. it relies on something like `X-Client-IP` or `Fastly-Client-IP` instead).

This should be handled by application code. Ratelimit can be configured to use a different header or application-level middleware can be added to set the `REMOTE_ADDR` value, it's up to the implementer.

Since I don't have a CCU I don't know how it behaves is such cases. I would suspect, that failures to communicate will result in a system message to appear. If I recall correctly, devices themselves have an option where you can configure a retry-count. So I expect the CCU does that, and if it fails, the system message is raised. And it would make sense if Homegear does it like that as well.
Assuming that I'm correct with what I've stated, people just using the CCU will have to react to the system message manually. At least if there's no hook for the scripts in the CCU to react on such events. So ultimately the "brain" in the setup (the human in this case) has to decide what to do.

So in case of your problem, it should not be too different from that. Although it would make sense if we could let HASS do the decision making. We already have the sum of system-messages available in HASS, and we do receive the log-message, that something has failed. So we would have to see what changses would have to be made to pyhomematic to allow HASS to assert the system messages in greater detail, and based on that information perform automations.

Just to make one thing clear: pyhomematic is essentially just a wrapper for bi-directional communication. Therefore I will in most cases I see it as the responsibility of HASS to perform tasks under certain conditions. HASS is the "smart" entity in the whole setup. And it should stay that way. Otherwise there could be future requests to bring more logic into pyhomematic even though HASS would be capable of performing these operations. It's like: Browser + HTTP + Server. If the browser fails to open a website because PHP is broken, you don't expect the HTTP protocol to work around the problem.

So to sum up my thoughts:
Which requirements do we have to meet within pyhomematic to enable HASS to reschedule setValue-operations if failures in the CCU or Homegear occur?
Mentioning @pvizeli just in case he has some Ideas, since he's deeply involved in HASS and probably knows best what would make sense.

Here is a one-line custom GUI that can be quickly added to a script that expects a filename as input.

```
import PySimpleGUI as sg
import sys

if len(sys.argv) == 1:
    rc, (fname,) = sg.FlexForm('My Script').LayoutAndRead([[sg.T('Document to open')], 
                                                           [sg.In(), sg.FileBrowse()],
                                                           [sg.Open(), sg.Cancel()]])
else:
    fname = sys.argv[1]

if not fname:
    sg.MsgBox("Cancel", "No filename supplied")
    raise SystemExit("Cancelling: no filename supplied")
```

(some rambling incoming, this post is largely for my own benefit, so I can get my thoughts consolidated)


This might be more difficult then I originally thought. For deleting default claims in the jwt, the problems boils down to two big parts:

1. How do you disable a claim
2. What do you do if a function is called or configuration value is set which requires one of the disabled claims.

Starting with number 2, as I think this will be the primary factor in how this feature is implemented. Lets look at some of the claims we put in the token (both in RFC 7519 spec and outside of the spec) and how they are used.

* ```identity```:
  * Returns the identity of a jwt via ```get_jwt_identity()```
* ```fresh```:
  * Allows marking endpoints as fresh with ```@fresh_jwt_required```
* ```type```:
  * Allows for making separate access and refresh tokens, and endpoints to match them via ```@jwt_required``` vs ```@jwt_refresh_token_required```
* ```jti```:
  * Used as a key for revoking tokens when ```app.config['JWT_BLACKLIST_ENABLED'] = True```
* ```user_claims```
  * Used to put custom claims into a token via ```@jwt.user_claims_loader``` and retrieved via ```get_jwt_claims()```. These are put into a seperate claim on the JWT to prevent key name collisions with our other custom keys we put in the jwt (such as fresh, type, identity).

Currently, when a jwt is decoded, we verify that all of the above claims are in the token, and the rest of the extension uses those claims as needed. If we remove one of the claims, it will remove some functionality from the extension, and we would need a way to signal the user of this if they attempted to use said functionality. For example, if a user disabled the ```type``` claim, and attempted to use ```@jwt_refresh_token_required```, I would like them to receive an exception along the lines of:

> Error: You are attempting to use jwt_refresh_token_required when the 'type' claim is disabled. To use this functionality, you must not disable the 'type' claim, and discard any current JWTs you have generated

compared to something like

> KeyError: 'type'

A way to do this might be to move the tokens from a straight python dictionary into a Tokens helper object, that raises good exceptions if a claim is attempted to be accessed that has actually been disabled. I haven't spent too much time thinking about this yet though, just my first thoughts.

This leads us into other problem, how do you disable a claim? We could expose the
```jwt_encode``` and ```jwt_decode``` methods to the user via a decorator, but the user would need to know the internal workings of this extension in order to safely re-write these functions in such a way that didn't cause problems when using other parts of the extension, such as ```@jwt_refresh_token_required``` or ```@fresh_jwt_required```. It would be totally do-able, and the easiest solution for me to implement, but it would also be the solution that would probably cause the most issues for a someone attempting to use it.

An alternate way would be to have a list of claims for our JWTs in the app.config, some functions to set the default values of the claims for a JWT, and decorators to override the default functions. We would need to have a way for various functions in flask-jwt-extended to see if a claim on a token was disabled or not too.

One problem I could see that would be common to any solution is what happens if you disable a claim, and want to re-add it later. If we initially have  ```fresh``` disabled, but
later decide that we want to use ```@fresh_jwt_required``` endpoints, we would need to re-enable the ```fresh``` claim. But in doing so, everyone who currently has a JWT now has a token that is missing that claim, and this extension would need to know how to handle that, or reject all of the existing JWTs as invalid (which probably isn't desired). This could get complex pretty quick.

Which leads us to one final consideration: this might add a decent complexity increase in the extension, and the less complex this extension is, the less chance there is for bugs (which, when working with an extension like this, any bug could have critical consequences). Is the ability to make a JWT a few bytes smaller worth this increase in complexity?

Anyways, there is my rambling of initial thoughts. I'll continue to think on this and update this ticket as needed.

That's a tough question that I think really depends on the requirements of your application. As a general thought, regardless of what you add in the `adding user context`, it probably cannot be 100% secure in the case of a stolen token. If you are basing it off an ip address, the attacker might have access to the same computer or it might be a different user on the NAT. If you are relying on additional headers about the browser configuration, they could be spoofed.

An idea that might be worth considering is only checking the refresh tokens against the blacklist, and have short lived access tokens that are secured with the additional user context. The refresh tokens shouldn't be used often (maybe once ever 5 to 10 minutes depending on how long your access token is valid for), so it shouldn't take many resources on your application to check it against a blacklist. Combining this with an access token that will expire in the next 5 to 10 minutes and is only valid from a specific ip should also limit the amout of damage that could be done if the access token is stolen. You still have to worry about what damage could be done if the refresh token is stolen, but thanks to the blacklist you have a way to revoke those tokens.

Another option (that can be used by itself or in conjunction with the above paragraph), is to use the 'token_freshness' pattern . This basically causes any access tokens created by the refresh token to be marked as not fresh, and you can use the `@fresh_jwt_required` decorator to prevent these tokens from accessing the most sensitive parts of your application (changing passwords, submitting credit cards, making payments, etc). You would also need to add another endpoint to your application that checked the users password, and if it was valid return to them a new fresh access token. This way if a fresh access token was stolen, it would only be able to do damage for 5~10 minutes until it expired, and if the refresh token was stolen they wouldn't be able to use that to access your most critical endpoints.

Hopefully these give you some ideas to think about. If you have any questions about this (or anything else), don't hesitate to let me know.

For testing purposes I used `pip install --no-cache-dir .` (which works) and I assumed it would work without the parameter as well. I was wrong as it seems!

- When using default way `pip install` the installer first looks for the `.so` and subsequently neglects to copy the file, since it wasn't there in the beginning.

- This is different when the "--no-cache-dir"  parameter is provided. The installer first builds the packages and then copies the corresponding files.


I don't see an fast and easy solution to that problem. The alternative would be to build the python package via cmake and then install via pip.

For now the easiest solution is probably to instruct people to use `pip install --no-cache-dir .`

I have made some tests based on your case, and for me everything works fine.
But here a few canveats.

- When changing the MIGRATIONS_MODULES, don't put the migrations for zinnia, into the directory 'migrations', can it will interfere with the current migrations of your app. Instead 'zinnia_migrations' is better and explicit.

- Before creating the migration for your new field, you should create a new initial migration for zinnia, that will reflect the state of Zinnia before your changes. 

- Then define ZINNIA_ENTRY_BASE_MODEL and create a your migration.

Note that applying this workflow, even if you update your installation on Zinnia, if new fields are provided, you just have to make a makemigrations, and you are OK.

Note if you have existing datas, after the creation of the initial migration, be sure to reset the migration state to Zinnia at 0001.

> and it's well-documented behaviour

Where is it documented? I honestly didn't even know they do this instead of `app.extensions`. Adding attributes to an object after it was created is sort of a bad practice anyway, even more so if it is done by a third party, so it's not something I would promote or recommend.

On the other side, the behavior of `sys.modules` is documented: https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/sys.html#sys.modules. I agree it is a bit hacky, but being documented means it is unlikely to change.

> It just seems much less hacky to me, checking an attribute of flask.current_app rather than checking sys.modules

My concern is that the Flask-Login author can change their mind at any time and stop adding this attribute to the app. This same person did not have any second thoughts about changing `is_authenticated` from a method to a property a couple of years ago, which caused pain to a lot of projects, several mine. So I don't really have any trust that this project has a strong sense of backwards compatibility.

```
# coding:utf-8

@dfarre This is actually because of the way that DRF constructs links to relations. I could think of a few things you could do:

* Create a subclass of `HyperlinkedRelatedField` and override the [`get_url` method](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/3.5.3/rest_framework/relations.py#L298) to exclude the format parameter.
* If you're using a subclass of `HyperlinkedRelatedSerializer`, then override the [`serializer_related_field` attribute](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/3.5.3/rest_framework/serializers.py#L1513), and set it to the `HyperlinkedRelatedField` subcless you created above.

**- OR -**

* Set CSV as the default format so that you don't have to include the format parameter

**- OR -**

* Request the resource with an [HTTP `Accept`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Content_negotiation#The_Accept_header) header set to `"text/csv"` so that you can omit the format parameter.

Looks like that both of your apps are running on the same device, this is not supported.

I think that KW is a bit misleading (the name and the documentation), it's not really switching between applications but actually switching between appium sessions. 
If you have both sessions pointing to the same device, the switching doesn't really do anything on the device itself, after the switch you just talk to the device using different appium server.
I could also imagine that this will cause problems with appium itself because those 2 appium servers try to talk to the same appium bootstrap on the device...

In short: the Apps must run on different devices.
- Supported: Device-A running App-1 and Device-B running App-2
- Supported: Device-A running App-1 and Device-B running App-1
- Not Supported: Device-A running App-1 and Device-A running App-2

For Example: you could test WhatsApp message sending from one device to another device. 

However it would be a cool feature to switch between applications on the same device. This should be possible using appium but would need a completely different implementation approach where the switching just brings the started app to the foreground.

It's just happened to me as well, but in my case it was a root Django logger that sent all messages above ERROR to some other handler (Sentry). daphne CLI uses logging to display errors, not stderr. When I reconfigured the logger to also write messages to the console, I started seeing the errors.
Also, daphne always exits with status code 0, even in case of an error. Which is an inconvenience if you start it from a batch file for example.

I need more information:

- Does this happen every single connection? Or just sometimes?
- What does your routing look like?
- What versions are you running of `daphne`, `channels`, `asgiref`, `channels-redis`, and `twisted`?

I am no twisted expert, but I suspect `_cleanup` is being called when the connection is closed, and hence this line is deleting the `self.channel` object: https://github.com/twisted/twisted/blob/trunk/src/twisted/web/http.py#L750

Here's what can be easily done:

- register a HUP signal listener
- close the Logger file handle on that signal
- rename the logfile from 'daphne.log' to 'daphne.log.1' (not sure about logrotate not already doing this)
- on the next logging event, Logger will automatically open a new file.

Then the logs can get rotated by sending a HUP signal to the daphne PID.

This may be tricky to fix because the Python YAML implementations are off-spec:

```
>>> import ruamel.yaml
>>> from pprint import pprint
>>> bools = "y|Y|yes|Yes|YES|n|N|no|No|NO|true|True|TRUE|false|False|FALSE|on|On|ON|off|Off|OFF".split("|") # boolean values according to the YAML spec
>>> bools = dict(zip(bools, [ruamel.yaml.load(b + ": 1") for b in bools]))
>>> pprint(bools)
{'FALSE': {False: 1},
 'False': {False: 1},
 'N': {'N': 1},
 'NO': {False: 1},
 'No': {False: 1},
 'OFF': {False: 1},
 'ON': {True: 1},
 'Off': {False: 1},
 'On': {True: 1},
 'TRUE': {True: 1},
 'True': {True: 1},
 'Y': {'Y': 1},
 'YES': {True: 1},
 'Yes': {True: 1},
 'false': {False: 1},
 'n': {'n': 1},
 'no': {False: 1},
 'off': {False: 1},
 'on': {True: 1},
 'true': {True: 1},
 'y': {'y': 1},
 'yes': {True: 1}}
```

Whereas with Go:

```
y map[true:1]
Y map[true:1]
yes map[true:1]
Yes map[true:1]
YES map[true:1]
n map[false:1]
N map[false:1]
no map[false:1]
No map[false:1]
NO map[false:1]
true map[true:1]
True map[true:1]
TRUE map[true:1]
false map[false:1]
False map[false:1]
FALSE map[false:1]
on map[true:1]
On map[true:1]
ON map[true:1]
off map[false:1]
Off map[false:1]
OFF map[false:1]
```

So files encrypted with the Python version that contain `y` or `n` as YAML keys will fail the MAC check. For the rest, the Go version should simply use the strings `True` or `False` for the MAC whenever a boolean is encountered.

Usually this happens when the number of timesteps in your audio is strictly smaller than the number of characters in the corresponding transcript.
If you have libdeepspeech installed as described in the installation instructions, you can easily use `deepspeech.utils.audioToInputVector` in a python script of your own to check each of your audio files and get the number of timesteps in it. You then just have to compare it to the number of characters in the corresponding transcript and delete those who comply to the rule `(n_chars < n_timesteps)`.

#149 catches `traitlets.config` up to use the new API (it would be a bit embarrassing to deprecate an API that traitlets itself still uses)

One aspect of the changes that I'm squeamish about is changing the signature of

```python
def _trait_changed(...):
    super()._trait_changed(...)
    other stuff
```

Which makes subclassing sensitive to these changes. We have to be careful about any classes where we are updating to use the new APIs and think about whether subclasses can reasonably override the signal methods. If they are, we have to update to the new APIs in backward-compatible ways, *even on the event listener methods*.

I asked myself the same question before I went ahead with the React
solution. I actually started to implement different jquery/react/angular
solution as add-on products.

Though, bottom line is that c.solr should provide a good out-of-the-box
solution and if we spread functionality across packages we will never
have one good solution. People will have to try different solutions
which will most likely be a frustrating experience. Most people just
don't care about the technology used and React is rather lightweight and
will be included in Plone core soon. Therefore we choosed React (even
though, I'm using much more Angular myself).

I think React is a good default choice today and we should rather work
on a common solution than having 10 different options (which we already
have with all the Solr and ES solution).

You can always ignore/override the search view, and we should indeed
document that. Though, we should provide a sane default way of doing
this (as we used to do before).

I know this is not consensus in the Plone community, but plain Browser
views are not an option any longer (for me personally). It is 2016 and
we should start to realize that...

We vendored `bind_sockets` and strategically placed some print statements. As per https://travis-ci.org/uber/tchannel-python/jobs/86301434, you'll note that `bind_sockets` gets called once and `sock.bind` (and incidentally `sock.listen`) twice: once with the IPv6 addr and once with the v4. The v6 one gets the OS assigned port and the v4 one then fails to bind with "Address already in use".

I'm experimenting with passing `family=socket.AF_INET` to `bind_sockets` to make it use IPv4 only for now.

Possibly relevant -- an epsilon is used in the CLI to allow round-tripping: https://github.com/mapbox/mercantile/blob/8971be31a7d9304409bd465b8292ab46dbe05c9e/mercantile/scripts/__init__.py#L282-L285

The challenge is that by definition, a given tile's (`[x, y, z]` lower right corner should == the upper left corner of tile `[x+1, y+1, z]`. By the same token, one might expect the same relationship with the lower right corner of `[x-1, y-1, z]`, but this tile is not returned here. However, I suspect that all of this is mercator math (https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Slippy_map_tilenames#Tile_numbers_to_lon..2Flat.), and we take the calculated UL corners of `[x, y, z]` + `[x+1, y+1, z]` to create the bounding box.

I can't see a great way to fix this beyond arbitrarily adjusting the precision of the bounds such that the x+1/y+1 tiles are not returned. @drwelby any thoughts on an approach?

This seems like another chance to mention Donald’s wonderful writeup on this:
https://caremad.io/posts/2013/07/setup-vs-requirement/

Donald was describing package dependencies, but the same principle applies for the dependency of *Python* itself. Pipfile (and requiremets.txt, the format it aims to replace) is for *applications* (webapps, bundled client applications, etc.), where you would only support one pinned version at a time. Information about pip-installable *libraries* should go into setup.py (or setup.cfg). In your case, your package seems to be intended to be installed by a non-controlled audience, using a variety of Python versions. This falls into the library category, and related metadata should therefore go into setup.py, not Pipfile.

Also, specifying `required.python_version` in Pipfile also does not really guarentee anything. With the proposed solution, your `pipenv check` calls on Travis CI would work, but there is still nothing preventing someone running a non-supported Python version from installing your package, because *Pip does not read Pipfile*. It reads setup.py and setup.cfg. To achieve your ultimate goal (preventing installation on incompatible versions), you need to put the requirement in setup.py or setup.cfg.

Now, like most packages out there contain both a setup.py and a requirements.txt, there is absolutely no problem have a Pipfile in your package repository, and use it via Pipenv. Pipenv itself does that as well. In that scenario, however, the Pipfile would simply manage the development environment. It does not have anything to say about how your package would be installed, and should not be used for that.

The password handling [happens in distutils](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/04e82934659487ecae76bf4a2db7f92c8dbe0d25/Lib/distutils/command/upload.py#L173). The username and password are passed unconditionally. Setuptools [supplements this behavior](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/0e62448b2674b3b321be28496b87362bc76ebcd1/setuptools/command/upload.py#L11-L42) to allow the username/password to be solicited at runtime (from keyring or prompt). And an empty value is considered the same as a None value.

> Is there no way to have a sitecustomize for every virtualenv?

No, there's no directory that gets added to ```sys.path``` "whenever you are in a virtualenv", but not when you're in a non-virtualenv environment.

You could probably put something together by using a global ```sitecustomize.py``` that checked if you were in a virtualenv (something like ```sys.prefix != sys.real_prefix```) and doing your "if I'm in a virtualenv" initialisation there. But again, that's just standard Python functionality. There's nothing specific that virtualenv does here.

If you want to call pip from Python and be sure you get the pip associated with the Python interpreter you're using, a much better way of doing so is

```python
subprocess.call([sys.executable, '-m', 'pip', "pip arguments here"])
```

That ensures you get the version of pip that you want, regardless of PATH or any other environment variables..

Looks like I figured it out. The two consumers were effectively not locking their partitions for fetching, since each consumer was using its own lock per partition. The fix just has the consumers share the same lock for each partition so that the locking logic actually works.

Can I add two related feature requests here for discussion?

I suppose a big use case for this feature is that people store their offsets outside of kafka. For that use case, in addition to manually setting, it would be nice to have a simple interface for getting offsets out of the consumer (this is different from `fetch_offsets` which gets them from kafka). I think this code in tests might be good enough for that? https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/24c364527012fe0768e2c6addc19be9990f3c1f6/tests/pykafka/test_simpleconsumer.py#L67

And, second, for this use case it would be good to have a third OffsetType, maybe named OffsetType:EXCEPTION or such, which would simply allow OffsetOutOfRangeError to bubble up to the application layer, rather than triggering a reset. That would let the user know that their external offset store needs checking.

Happy to work on either of those, if we think it's a good idea.

Moving forward with swagger.py": my company project is making use of cornice 0.17 and we're using `@resource` for all our resources ("paths" in swagger speak).  I have put effort into revising swagger.py in our local environment to be 2.0 compatible instead of 1.2 (which was the aim of 0.17's swagger.py).  I would be very happy to contribute to the development of the cornice swagger module.  Is there a dev branch i can help with here?   Has there been design discussion on swagger.py so I can implement in a particular way?

## Proposed implementation
So far I have been running forward like so (_italics_ mean it's a Swagger term, and `code` means it's python):
Our swagger.py has a modified `generate_swagger_spec()` and some decorators.  Essentially, we can add data to the swagger document: `@swagger.path` at the _path_ level, and `@swagger.operation' at the _operation_ level.  These hooks to add swagger data would merge with data harvested by introspection (ie  _summary_ comes from each resource method's `__doc__`).   

The swagger spec offers globals (ie [_path-level parameters_](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-spec/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#fixed-fields-4) which can be defined, implying every _operation_ in that _path_ requires the same parameters.  Per the linked doc, such _parameters_ can be overwritten, but not removed from the list of required parameters of all the _operations_ which inherit them.    Using this same rule of overwriting, we can can use this design for swagger.py:
1. The new signature will be `generate_swagger_spec(services, title, version, **kwargs)`
  * These args are the _Required_ fields for a [_Swagger Object_](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-spec/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#swagger-object)
  * `**kwargs` can supply any other _Swagger Object_ parameter by name (_host_, _parameters_, etc)
2. Swagger data will be acquired in two ways:
  * Introspection of cornice and pyramid related code
    - _path_ parameters come from `cornice.service.path`
    - _operation_ key values (get, put, etc) in a [_Path Item Object_](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-spec/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#path-item-object) come from `cornice.service.definitions`
  * Addition using the decorators
    - `@swagger.path` takes `**kwargs` which map to [_Pattern Item Object_](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-spec/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#path-item-object) values (only _parameters_ are useful I think)
    - `@swagger.operations` takes `**kwargs` which will map completely to [_Operation Object_](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-spec/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#operationObject)
3. Overwrite rule:
  * Introspection-harvested data happens first
  * Added (decorated) data gets used to add/overwrite (using `dict.udpate`)


## Example
### Decorated Resources
Such a resource would be found with `pyramid.config.scan()`
```python
from my_swagger_models import parameter_definitions
@resource(collection_path='/tokens', path='/tokens/{id}')
@swagger.path(parameters=parameter_definitions)
class Token(object):
    """A token is an object of my appreciation"""
    def __init__(self, request):
        self.request = request

    # This method implements the GET /tokens/<id> http call.
    def get(self):
        """What what you got"""
...
   # This method implements the POST /tokens http call.
   # These Swagger       **kwargs map directly to Operation Object Definitions
    @swagger.operation(parameters=[{'name': 'X-OpenAM-Username', 'in': 'header', 'description': 'LDAP Username'},
                                       {'name': 'X-OpenAM-Password', 'in': 'header', 'description': 'LDAP Password'}])
    def collection_post(self):
...
```


### The View
```python
# We can store our global details elsewhere, or maybe generate them from some other plugin to get a swagger-compliant nested dictionary
from my_swagger_models import parameters, responses

@view_config(route_name='swagger_json', renderer='json', )
def swagger_json(request):
    '''Swagger spec in JSON form'''
    services = service.get_services()
    # We can even define swagger compliant dictionaries for any other Swagger Object item
    info = {'title': 'NMS API', 'version': '1.0', 'contact': {
        'name': 'Hamx0r',
        'email': 'hamx0r@github.com'}
        }

    return generate_swagger_spec(services, info['title'], info['version'], info=info, parameters=parameters, responses=responses)
```

My goal is to offload documentation to the well-maintained swagger spec documentation by keeping a 1:1 mapping off terminology and structure (which works great since nested Python dicts/lists look very  JSONic).  Also, it allow creative uses of the decorators by users who may want to override or add documentation notes, or compile such notes by introspecting other code they may be using (ie cliquet).

The new test failures are due to a race.  When doing load_before there's some code that looks like:

                future.set_result(data)
                if data:
                    data, start, end = data
                    self.cache.store(oid, start, end, data)

We notify the future before putting data in the cache. Some tests are effectively waiting on the future and are checking the cache size before the cache is updated.

What's especially weird:

- Only Python 2.7

- Not on MacOS

Some difference in thread scheduling I guess.

I could "fix" this by notifying the the future after updating the cache, but it seems slightly better to notify the future sooner, given that updating the cache potentially takes some meaningful time, because it's doing I/O.  

This makes the test code a little awkward. Someone might argue that as things are now, the cache data are inconsistent with the load result, but really the cache is an implementation detail, except when doing whitebox tests.

Thoughts?

Thanks @cancan101 and everyone else :+1:   However my situation was a little different since we had no use for the session store or `django.contrib.auth` -- we are using a lightweight version of django as a microservice for managing user authentication. However, we had 2 views that were non-api that depended on the `@login_required` decorator and they would fail obviously due to the `AnonymousUser` issue mentioned above. FYI we are also using django for all the other services but using builtin authorization so the service doesn't need to reach out to the users/auth service each time. 

Ergo, I borrowed some of @cancan101 's code and made some tweaks. 

```python
from django.utils.functional import SimpleLazyObject
from django.contrib.auth.models import AnonymousUser

from rest_framework.request import Request
from rest_framework_jwt.authentication import JSONWebTokenAuthentication


def get_user_jwt(request):
    """
    Replacement for django session auth get_user & auth.get_user
     JSON Web Token authentication. Inspects the token for the user_id,
     attempts to get that user from the DB & assigns the user on the
     request object. Otherwise it defaults to AnonymousUser.

    This will work with existing decorators like LoginRequired  ;)

    Returns: instance of user object or AnonymousUser object
    """
    user = None
    try:
        user_jwt = JSONWebTokenAuthentication().authenticate(Request(request))
        if user_jwt is not None:
            # store the first part from the tuple (user, obj)
            user = user_jwt[0]
    except:
        pass

    return user or AnonymousUser()


class JWTAuthenticationMiddleware(object):
    """ Middleware for authenticating JSON Web Tokens in Authorize Header """
    def process_request(self, request):
        request.user = SimpleLazyObject(lambda : get_user_jwt(request))
```

This forces all requests to have a valid JWT before they can access any of the DRF api resources __with the added benefit__ of still functioning properly with the `@login_required` decorator.  Ergo, this makes adding something like django-oauth-toolkit a cinch (with a little customization to make it return JWT instead of a stored standard token). 

We have actually built a very lightweight version of django `skeleton` that we have used for many microservices - each using separate datastores - to much success. I'm actually very impressed with the performance. 

If anyone has any questions, suggestions, or improvements I'd love to hear them. 

Thanks. 

P.S. @jpadilla Is there a chance we can get something like this added into the core package as an option for those users who depend on `request.user` to an actual `User` instance vs `AnonymousUser`  - I know its not relative to all cases but it would be very useful. I'd be happy to write the docs and answer any questions about how we're using django for microservices very successfully.

I'm not sure that the above fix completely solves the problem with Python 3+  It does solve the import error, but I believe there are other issues with the `argument_parser.CsvListSerializer` class (like, `unicode` doesn't exist in python3 and it's mentioned within the `Serialize` function.)
```
class CsvListSerializer(ArgumentSerializer):

  def __init__(self, list_sep):
    self.list_sep = list_sep

  def Serialize(self, value):
    """Serialize a list as a string, if possible, or as a unicode string."""
    output = cStringIO.StringIO()
    writer = csv.writer(output)

    # csv.writer doesn't accept unicode, so we convert to UTF-8.
    encoded_value = [unicode(x).encode('utf-8') for x in value]  ### <- broken in python3
    writer.writerow(encoded_value)
```

Honestly, looking at the class right above this one - I'm not sure I understand why this wouldn't work instead for either major version of python:
```
CsvListSerializer = ListSerializer
```
I say this because the `CsvListSerializer` _already_ ignores the `list_sep` argument and the ListSerializer uses the helper function to convert the list values into unicode strings.  

Unless I'm missing something, the `CsvListSerializer` class is basically useless and `ListSerializer(',')` should be used instead.

This would prevent the need to import `cStringIO` in this module entirely.

I will comment again: @FortAwesome (@davegandy and @tagliala), this is now almost three years old, is the sixth most commented on open issue in this repository, and there have been ten releases since this issue was opened. Most people (like me) on this thread are moochers; we have no way in assisting with the design of the icons. However, as an open source and *extremely* popular project (you should be proud of that), this really ought to get moving.

Services like Fonticons and Fontello do technically solve the problem, but they add an extra step, often add unnecessary total file sizes, make automatic download/build/compile workflows a nightmare, cost money and can even change the implementation of the font (different prefixes or positioning).

Thank you so much for your hard work on this literally awesome project. The challenge is finding one with the right license. I use getAllowedValues() on two attributes: Milestones and a custom field we call Products. I don't know the exact type of the Products field, but in the Rally web interface, it is selected from a drop-down list of multiple string values. Over time, new values are added to the list. When retrieved using pyral, the field shows as a COLLECTION.

Walking through the pyral code, I noticed that getAllowedValues() did retrieve the allowed values for Products, but short-circuited before returning them. Instead, it returns a single-item list of True. I wrote a workaround that is based on the pyral getAllowedValues() code, but doesn't short circuit. The workaround code is in a comment in Issue #99 

To retrieve the Milestones values, I have to use a different workaround implemented in the examples/get_milestones.py code provided here on Github.

I would guess that getAllowedValues() is not a method that's called very often--probably a max of once or twice, like in my script. As such, it might not be worthwhile to attempt to improve its performance by skipping "unwanted" values (especially if those values turn out to be wanted). Even if getAllowedValues() is an expensive method, I suspect it would still take up only a small portion of the total execution time when combined with Rally.get() and other pyral methods that a typical script would call. But I'm just guessing. Did users complain about long getAllowedValues() times?

Chalice uses `requirements.txt` is used to determine what third party packages to bundle up with application and deploy to lambda as documented [here](http://chalice.readthedocs.io/en/latest/topics/packaging.html#rd-party-packages).

By freezing your dev environment to `requirements.txt` you are telling it to ship everything in your dev environment to your lambda runtime environment, some of which can't easily be packaged such as `typing`. 

Removing typing from `requirements.txt` doesn't fix the issue because you left `chalice` in which takes typing as a dependency so you are not removing the dependency from what it tries to package since it finds all the transitive dependencies. If you would like to have development requirements then put them in something like `requirements-dev.txt`, those should be totally different from your `requirements.txt` which is what you want to end up in the deployed lambda function.

I am unable to reproduce the hanging issue. It just deploys without `typing` when I try to repro on Chalice 1.0.1

One hack you could do is using the _get_result function from testresources (from testresources import _get_result), which essentially traverses up the stack looking for the test result object.

At that point in your TearDown you can probably do something like:

```python

if not result.wasSuccessful():
    return # i.e. don't do any cleanup
```

The testresources package would be a more appropriate choice for managing this kind of testresources (like a VM), and in theory it should handle your use case via TestResource.finishedWith(), which gets passed the test result object.

However, bear in mind that while testresources is very powerful, it is also a bit complex and has some rough edges. For example:

https://bugs.launchpad.net/testresources/+bug/1506476

might affect you (although you might not need nested resources).

> its a dis-service to developers to make them buy the whole house when they only want the kitchen

This is actually tricky. The majority of people in UX studies want the whole house. The more advanced users eventually run into a problem and decide they only want the kitchen -- but only after trying out lots of things and using gcloud for a while...

So, we have a bit of an issue: do we optimize for the advanced folks? If we do, we might alienate and frustrate users, effectively reducing the number of people entering the funnel who could eventually become those advanced folks...

My thought here is still that we need to make both possible, but make the common case the simplest: `pip install gcloud` gives me the house. If I want just the kitchen, or just the light switch, this should be *possible* but not necessarily something we optimize for. Does that make sense?

@kir-titievsky, @theacodes We currently test for an overflow of the message count *after* appending the message in `publisher.batch.thread.Batch.publish`.  I think the "overflow" logic needs to be normalized for both `max_bytes` and `max_messages`:

- First, remove exceptions for individual messages exceeding `max_bytes` in both `publisher.batch.base.Batch.will_accept` or in `publisher.client.Client.publish`.
- Then, inside `publisher.batch.thread.Batch.publish`

  1. Test for overflow of *either* bytes or message count *before* appending the message to the batch.
  2. If either is true, *and* we already have messages, spawn the commit thread for the current batch and return `None` (forcing the client to create a new batch).  The client with then publish the message via the new batch.
  3. If there is overflow, but there are no prior messages in the batch, go ahead and append the message, trigger the commit thread, and return the future for the message (the overflowing message will go out in its own request).
  4. If there is no overflow, append the message and return its future.

As an alternative to step 3, we could just remove the existing post-append overflow test (currently only for count), and let the monitor thread or the next call to `publish` trigger the commit.

```python
>>> blob.media_link
'https://www.googleapis.com/download/storage/v1/b/{BUCKET}/o/{OBJECT}?generation=15072
26993155365&alt=media'
```

Second, I'm not sure why it'd fail. GCS writes are either done in a single request (for small payloads / if you don't set the chunk size) or via a resumable upload which has a unique identifier that gets sent with every chunk (and each chunk must identify it's byte range when being uploaded). While two uploads might overlap, they'd end up pointing to different destinations.

to me:

#1 is concerned with "am I reading the proper payload from the server"
#2 is concerned with "is the payload I read from the server, whether properly retrieved or not, a valid dist"

it seems entirely possible that a given server can serve up an invalid dist as it's proper payload (i.e. we could not clearly indicate a connection/fetch issue based on a validation failure). this should not be a retryable event - and as we can see pex already blows up on it, just post-fetch. it's not clear to me what problem we would be solving by just moving that validation to fetch-time vs resolve-time.

but if the reason the dist is malformed is because the server didn't send all of it, I think that's certainly a retryable event (and/or at least one we can give a much better error message about) and ultimately the core problem I think we want to solve for here. I'm not sure there's a better way to do that in the framework of HTTP other than to find some reliable way to check the Content-Length in `urllib{,3}`?

> ('select', SelectKBest(chi2, k=5000))

You're working with a "bag of words" text classification model, which operates on 5000 keywords.

Scikit-Learn calculates these 5000 keyword contributions by doing a single "big" function invocation, whereas (J)PMML does the same by doing 5000 "small" function invocations. The cost of text operations between Python and Java should be comparable(?), so the ten times performance decrease should be solely attributable to function invocation overhead.

You could try repeating this experiment with 500, 2'000 and 10'000 keywords and report back your findings. I expect the performance decrease to be less of an issue with smaller keyword sets, and more of an issue with bigger keyword sets.

If this performance decrease is unacceptable for your use case, then:
1. Limit the size of keyword sets from 5'000 to something (significantly-) smaller.
2. Just keep using Scikit-Learn.

> Is there support of LinearSVC in sklearn2PMML?

Not yet. See https://github.com/jpmml/jpmml-sklearn/issues/56

> Can we do evaluation of a pmml model in java using batches?

(J)PMML is designed to operate on individual data records.

You can group data records in your application in whatever way you want, but the JPMML-Evaluator library will still score them one by one. Conceptually, it is possible to extend the JPMML-Evaluator library to perform "vectorized operations" in specific cases, but fully building out this capability is not a priority at the moment.
