@davelab6 just asked me if we could run fontmake in parallel on the Google Cloud Platform, since I'm doing something similar for Font Bakery right now.

Sure, we could, it would need some modifications though, but we could reuse or extend what we have. It would probably build one instance per pod. Similarly, one quick improvement on a local machine would be to just spawn one fontmake process per instance to create. The kernel would take care of distributing this to different processor cores.

It would be nice though, to run the work within an instance build in parallel, i.e. map per glyph, interpolate and then reduce to the new font, on a multiprocessor machine that would be awesome. 

Still, my guess is that something fishy is going on in that code, I believe it should be much faster judging from what it does. So, a thorough performance review may help as well.

> It would be great if you could help us identify where the bottlenecks really are.

Yeah, that's the hard part :-) Usually it's either moving calls to functions out of a loop, to execute just before or to do some memoization (=buy speed with memory) etc. Caching is another form of hell though.

The problem is, a performance review is time-consuming since you need to get to know the code very well. About profiling to identify bottlenecks, I remember reading a blog post (which I can't find anymore of course). The author used to interrupt the running program at random points in time and claimed that the performance critical code would be very often where the program was at that moment, because that's where most time is spent. So he would take a deep look at these common locations and start optimizing there. This is a bit brutal and dirty, but not a really bad idea. For python, this would mean to start fontmake and then hit `Ctrl+c` while it's running. This will produce a `KeyboardInterrupt` with a full stack trace. I never tried this, but I would give it a shot in this case :grin:

The team_context specifies which team to get the backlog for.  If you want to get the backlog for the default team for the project, you can specify one of the project properties with the name or id of the project, and leave the team fields as None.  If you want the backlog for a specific team in your project then you will need to specify both a project in one of the project properties and the team in one of the team properties.   The team_context model has redundant properties as a fallout from the code generation tool.  The source of the model is C# where the `_id` fields expect GUIDs.  The fields without `_id` are strings in c#.   In python they are both strings, so the model does not translate well.  The important thing when using the python sdk is that one of the project properties should always be set in a team context, and one of the team properties may or may not be set depending on whether you want the default team or a specific team.  

`    :param project: The team project Id or name.  Ignored if ProjectId is set.`
`    :param project_id: The Team Project ID.  Required if Project is not set.`
`    :param team: The Team Id or name.  Ignored if TeamId is set.`
`    :param team_id: The Team Id`

Required:  Set either project or project_id
Optional: Set either team or team_id

`r'73[0-9a-f]{40}3014'` is EVM bytecode for

```
PUSH20 <address>
ADDRESS
EQ
```

I.e., a comparison of a constant `<address>` (determined by the constructor at deployment time), with the actual address in the current execution context.

I think this relates to this change introduced in [`solc` v0.4.20](https://github.com/ethereum/solidity/releases/tag/v0.4.20):

> Code Generator: Prevent non-view functions in libraries from being called
directly (as opposed to via delegatecall).

Discussed with @mingwandroid.  Problem here is that linux file systems are case-sensitive by default, and macOS is case-insensitive by default.  So for working with linux-64 conda package on macOS, you're going to have to provide a case-sensitive volume for conda to interact with.

Besides setting `CONDA_SUBDIR`, you're also going to need to set `CONDA_PKGS_DIRS` and point it at a path on that case-sensitive volume.  And then of course `--prefix` is going to have to be on that volume as well.

Keeping this issue open so that we can raise a better error message when this happens.

[path.repo](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/2.3/breaking_20_snapshot_and_restore_changes.html#breaking_20_snapshot_and_restore_changes) must be set in your `elasticsearch.yml` on _each_ master and/or data node.

After you've set this (and the user running Elasticsearch _must_ have read/write privileges for that file system/path) and restarted Elasticsearch, then you should be able to create the repository with that command.

That command-line syntax will _not_ work with 4.  You will need to read up on the 4.0 docs: https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/4.0/index.html

This kind of timeout can happen when ES is heavily overburdened.  In this case, it appears you're trying to delete many indices at once (could you elaborate? provide an approximate count?), which may contribute to that overburdening.  An increased timeout value will likely help with this.  Reducing the number of indices being deleted simultaneously might also help.  This could be accomplished by reducing the time window (`--older-than X`, and slowly reduce X until it is the desired number).

If you hardly have any indices (seeing that you're deleting older than 3), this might also indicate an overburdened cluster (or single node).

As far as reporting the error goes, I made a deliberate choice not to complicate the code here.  Trying to capture one of multiple potential errors here, and responding correctly to each kind, is a lot of extra code for each method making calls–especially when trying to make both Python 2.x and 3.x play nice with the same code base.  Since `--debug` output will show the actual error, I leave it to the end user to use `--debug` when they hit errors.

https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/documentation/articles/service-bus-amqp-python/

The ServiceBus team were not able to propose better performance using HTTP, due to HTTP limit.

@cosbyr Yes - we've removed the get_account_info in the latest versions due to changes in how we report back the usage/limits. There isn't any replacement at the moment - especially for retrieving the prediction key programmatically. The recommendation would be to add the prediction key to your project vs retrieve it programmatically.

1.2 REST API continues to function and there's no immediate plans to deprecate it, if that changes we'll communicate it with time to migrate off.

The first WS message is `0x81 0x06 0x22 0x78 0x78 0x78 0x78 0x22`.
It means `0x81 0x06` header and `"xxxx"` data.
0x81 means a single text message (FIN + TEXT flags), 0x06 means 6 bytes of data.
The message is correct according to RFC: https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6455#section-5.2

I have no idea why `wscat` assumes that `0x6` is *opcode*, the opcode is stored in the first byte and it is 0x1 (TEXT).

Please note that browsers process sending a message just after WS handshake correctly: https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/examples/web_ws.py#L22

Ok, what I ended up doing was just removing the line for setting `Base.query`. I think that's not actually necessary -- it just gives you the ability to do things like `Assignment.query.all()` rather than `db.query(Assignment).all()`. But you're right, you might want to create connections to e.g. other databases, in which case it's better to always do that through the explicit db object.

Yup, I think that makes good sense as a goal. Where we have some trouble is that the traitlets config system doesn't perform any merging of the values of individual traits, it only merges at the configurable-object level (i.e. it merges user_config.Config with sys_config.Config, but doesn't merge the contents of user_config.Config.value with sys_config.Config.value). While this isn't desirable for extension loading, it is most of the time. In some ways, this points to the traitlets config system simply being a bad fit for extensions, as we rapidly expand the scope of what configuration we are talking about enabling.

@SylvainCorlay discussed at the meeting a conf.d trait-type that would implement the directory-style loading without requiring changes to how traitlets.config works.

Alternately, we could move the serverextensions piece to use the exact same code as nbextensions, which would ensure the behavior of the two is totally consistent, rather than using traitlets.config for one and frontend-config for the other. Right now, I'm leaning a bit toward this option.

I think the nbextensions case is just a bug where the load order is backwards. That should be readily fixable.

Very cool tool, though for me the best of this kind remains [LyX](http://www.lyx.org). It lets you combine typing LaTeX that gets auto-rendered (like MathQuill) with having command palettes for more esoteric stuff you may not know/remember how to type in LaTeX.  

That makes it incredibly powerful: if you know the commands, you just type along and it simply "compiles on the fly" to WYSIWYM ("What you see is what you mean", i.e. close enough visually to the final LaTeX render to be useful, but not pixel-perfect so it can be done on the fly without a full LaTeX call). But if you don't know something, you can use the palettes to visually create the piece you are missing.  When I was doing lots more complex mathematical writing, it saved me endless amounts of poring over LaTeX docs, and I even used it often to answer colleagues' questions about how to do something in LaTeX: I would just open LyX, type/build up what they needed, and then view the raw LaTeX (which it will show you if you want) for the answer to copy/paste into a raw `.tex` file.

I've yet to find a system for math editing that I like better than LyX.

Well, the issue you encounter here, is that having exactly the same rendering is expected in many case, and is what users want to have. %%HTML is a [minimal wrapper](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blob/master/IPython/core/magics//display.py#L49-L52) around the display protocol. You can if you like redefine it to wrap your output in a div with a border to make it clear: ``` from IPython.display import HTML, display from IPython.core.magic import register_cell_magic @register_cell_magic def html(line, cell): display(HTML("
"+cell+"
")) ```
Thus far my deployment hasn't run into any trouble, users have been pleasantly surprised.

Okay, so this thing was left in limbo because no one ever provided any feedback, and then I had to step away for a while. Here's a summary of the issue so far. I'll try to keep this as brief as possible while including enough details.

Around the time I started this thread, I identified three strategies to distinguish between `None` and "not set". Eventually, I realized that the initial approach (visible `Undefined` objects) was a bad idea because the expression `x is None` would counterintuitively evaluate to `False` when `x == Undefined`. A lot of existing code would silently break.

That left two alternatives:

### Pythonic strategy ###

From an application's point of view, **unset fields would behave much like non-existent attributes on any regular Python object**. (Internally, though, `_data` might be populated with `Undefined` objects for bookkeeping.)

```python
class Foo(Model):
    somefield = StringType()
```

```python
>>> instance = Foo()
>>> instance.somefield
AttributeError: 'Foo' object has no attribute 'somefield'
```

To assist with backward-compatibility, there would be an easy way to auto-initialize empty fields to `None` so as to match current behavior. Strategies (not mutually exclusive) include global or model-level options, a `Model.__init___` argument, and a dedicated method to populate an instance's empty fields at will.

To check if a field is set, you'd use `hasattr(instance, 'somefield')`, as with any object, or the dict-like interface `'somefield' in instance`, which is already supported.

`Model.get(fieldname)` would return a field's value or `None` if the field is empty. So, in cases where you don't care about the unset/`None` distinction, `instance.get('somefield')` would be equivalent to `instance.somefield` today.

### Tack-on strategy ###

Superficially, things would work like now. `None` would still be returned when an unset field is accessed, but **an application could explicitly check if a `None` actually means `None` or if it means "not set".**

```python
>>> instance = Foo()
>>> print(instance.somefield)
None
>>> isset(instance, 'somefield')
False
```

```python
>>> instance.somefield = 1
>>> isset(instance, 'somefield')
True
>>> instance.somefield = None
>>> isset(instance, 'somefield')
True
```

```python
>>> del instance.somefield
>>> isset(instance, 'somefield')
False
```

(The above examples use a global `isset` function, but it could be a `Model` method as well.)

### Evaluation ###

Personally I would be fine with either alternative.

The tack-on strategy is appealing in that it seems to be 100% backward-compatible, with the obvious exception of code that manipulates internals like `_data` directly. Regular applications can start using the feature right away with no changes to existing code (as far as I can work out).

The pythonic strategy can also be implemented selectively in an existing codebase using the compatibility helpers I mentioned. However, once you have a model that follows the new paradigm, you can't put it through code that blindly accesses the model's fields without checking first (or using `.get()`), as that may raise an `AttributeError`.

Thoughts?

Once we have even a tentative decision, I can deliver a draft PR pretty quickly. If no one comments, then I'll decide. :grin:

Some, I did some digging, and I think this might be a problem not with the `MultilpleModelAPIView`, but with your router config.  Here's the relevant lines from the [Django Rest Framework documentation](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/routers/):


> Note: The base_name argument is used to specify the initial part of the view name pattern. In the example above, that's the user or account part.

> Typically you won't need to specify the base_name argument, but if you have a viewset where you've defined a custom get_queryset method, then the viewset may not have a .queryset attribute set. If you try to register that viewset you'll see an error like this:

> ``` 
> 'base_name' argument not specified, and could not automatically determine the name from the viewset, as it does not have a '.queryset' attribute.
> ```
> This means you'll need to explicitly set the base_name argument when registering the viewset, as it could not be automatically determined from the model name. 

Since the `MultipleModelAPIView` doesn't establish a queryset attribute (it doesn't use it), you'll need to manually specify you're `base_name` when adding it to a router.  Let me know if that catches the problem.

There is now a new project: https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-ci


Since we wanted to keep the `ci` folder, and that `git subtree` could not (easily) be used to do so, history has been instead filtered/extracted from scikit-build using  `dockgit-rocket-filter`

[jcfr/dockgit-rocket-filter](https://github.com/jcfr/dockgit-rocket-filter) which is a dockerized version of [GitRocketFilter](https://github.com/xoofx/GitRocketFilter).


For reference, here were the steps:

```
git clone git@github.com:scikit-build/scikit-build.git scikit-ci
cd scikit-ci

# Extract
git branch -M oldmaster
git-rocket-filter --branch master --keep ci
git branch -D oldmaster

# Clean
git remote remove origin
git tag -l | xargs git tag -d
git reflog expire --expire=now --all
git gc --aggressive --prune=now

# Add README and LICENSE

# Publish
git remote add origin git@github.com:scikit-build/scikit-ci.git
git push origin master
```

[This comment](https://github.com/SatelliteQE/nailgun/issues/41#issuecomment-94506248) discusses an approach for making method signatures. It provides these two method signatures as an example:

```python
def add_subscriptions(self, params):
def content_override(self, content_label, value):
```

That comment then goes on to discuss why the latter method signature is preferred. In short:

* The latter method is more discoverable. A user can simply look at the possible arguments, or even the docstring for that method, and have an idea about what information the method requires.
* The latter method allows NailGun to shield users from design quirks (such as the API requiring a nested hash) or bugs (such as the API redundantly requiring an entity ID).

There's one challenge in this approach that has not yet been discussed. The challenge is wrapping calls such as `PUT /fusor/api/v21/deployments/:id/deploy`. [Currently,](https://github.com/SatelliteQE/nailgun/pull/98#issuecomment-107681829)

> the deploy method only accepts one optional parameter, but could be expanded to more in future builds. Also, the update method can be used to update any deployment value

In other words, explicitly listing parameters when those parameters are *optional* and *variable* is problematic. It doesn't make sense to write this:

```python
def deploy(foo=None, bar=None, biz=None):
```

…given that those arguments may change in the future, and given that passing `foo=null` to the server is different than not passing `foo` at all.

The currently adopted solution is to just accept a dict of parameters that are passed straight to the server. However, accepting a dict of parameters and handing them straight to the server is not ideal for all the reasons summarized at the top of this post. It's also not ideal because we're adopting two very different approaches for passing in arguments to methods: passing in a dict and explicitly naming arguments.

One solution is to accept a list of kwargs for methods such as `deploy()`:

```python
def deploy(**kwargs):
```

This has the advantage of letting the call syntax be similar for all helper methods.

I authored the AES 128 vs 256 part 2 years ago and it doesn't seem like the state of the art has changed since. While the performance difference is probably not the highest concern, there has been some research that suggest that attacks on AES256 are easier to achieve than on the 128 variant. The reasoning is that most attack on AES do not directly target the rounds or the key length, but focus more on side channels like timing attacks. If AES 128 is more resistant to those than 256, then 128 should be preferred. This thread has more information: http://www.mail-archive.com/dev-tech-crypto@lists.mozilla.org/msg11325.html

I'm happy to explain more about the reasoning behind each level if you'd like. We spend a lot of time making sure the levels are current and support the security and compatibility needs of old, intermediate and modern web applications. It would be a great honor to have them included in letsencrypt!

This came up during troubleshooting of an issue with SickRage's date handling while trying to display a TV show's air times by the timezone of a network.  SickRage is running with Python 2.7.10 but I have confirmed this with a simple test on Python 2.7.10 and Python 3.4.3.

When the TV show's network is None, the SickRage code works on Debian but not Windows (tested on Win 10 x64, Win 8.1 x64, Win XP x86 SP3).  In SickRage, `network_timezones.py` sets a variable 'sb_timezone=tz.tzlocal()' as a fallback when the network's timezone was `None` or could not be parsed.  This seemed to be the failing point on Windows Systems.

So, while looking at 'dateutil' to see how Windows differed, I found that in 'tz.py' during the import ?(shown below) `tzwin` was never being imported due to an ImportError in `tzwin.py` that has since been fixed in dateutil (with `from six.moves import winreg').  
```python
try:
    from dateutil.tzwin import tzwin, tzwinlocal
except (ImportError, OSError):
    tzwin, tzwinlocal = None, None
``` 
SickRage updated `dateutil` from version 2.2 to 2.4.2. but the error persisted.  So I looked at the changes to `dateutil` and found that `tzwin.py` still wasn't importing which can be verified with
```python
import dateutil.tz as tz
print(tz.tzwinlocal)
```
Which should output `<class 'dateutil.tzwin.tzwinlocal'>` but instead returns `None`
The cause is a failure import `tzname_in_python2() ` due to the above mentioned exception.  However on normal usage of dateutil you don't import `tzwin.py` directly and the exception gets swallowed during the `tz.py` try:except clause during import of `tzwin`

ref: (SiCKRAGETV/sickrage-issues/issues/3007)

This problem was almost certainly introduced in PR #22. It's easy enough to patch over (just don't check whether or not there are hours if the fuzzy flag is enabled), but it does raise a bit of a problem which is that the AM/PM flag is being erroneously set anytime an "a" shows up in a fuzzy parsing situation.

Personally, I think a lot of these parser issues are coming from the over-broad / under-specified nature of the parsing. It's very hard to make any changes without removing support for some weird edge case. The main reason I haven't closed issue #26 yet is that I'd like to add an enumeration of the supported formats so that we can explicitly detect those patterns. The fact of the matter is that no well-formatted date uses "a" to mean "AM" in any context *except* immediately following a number, except if you are parsing some slang-y fuzzy date string like, "At 10 in the a"

For now I'll just add in the kludge-y patch since even though it's just putting a band-aid over this particular problem, it's not doing any long-term harm.

from dateutil import rrule as rr
from dateutil import datetime as dt

I can confirm this error using anaconda 4.4.2 on OS X 10.11.6 with XCode installed. I solved the error by removing both shapely and cartopy. 

Depending on your install you may need to do the following:
```
conda remove shapely
conda remove cartopy
```
or 
```
pip uninstall cartopy
pip uninstall shapely
```

This is followed by 
`pip install shapely cartopy --no-binary shapely --no-binary cartopy`

If you use shapely or cartopy installed from conda you will end up with the same error and have to start over again. 

This is confirmed to work on OS X, Linux Mint, and Windows https://github.com/SciTools/cartopy/issues/738

@niltonvasques You don’t have weather segment there:

1. `powerline.segments.common.wthr` is a module name, *not* function name.
2. You must put module name *and* function name in `function` key.
3. `name` key specifies *segment* name and it is better not to use it. This name no longer has anything to do with segment function.
4. It is highly suggested to run `powerline-lint` prior to reporting anything here. Let's look at your example of adding an empty list to an SArray. So, this SArray will be a list where each element represents an image. If I re-write what you had as one element per line, it looks like this:
```
train_data2_annotations = [
  {'coordinates': {'y': 32, 'x': 147, 'width': 290, 'height': 67}, 'label': 'Sneaker'}, 
  {'coordinates': {'y': 1124, 'x': 1777, 'width': 105, 'height': 265}, 'label': 'Sneaker2'}, 
  [],
]
```
Every element must itself be a list. So, if you intended for this SArray to hold the information for three images, the last one being a pure negative, it should have the following format:
```
train_data2_annotations = [
  [{'coordinates': {'y': 32, 'x': 147, 'width': 290, 'height': 67}, 'label': 'Sneaker'}], 
  [{'coordinates': {'y': 1124, 'x': 1777, 'width': 105, 'height': 265}, 'label': 'Sneaker2'}], 
  [],
]
```

I pushed a fix that does more extensive normalization of inputs during create() and set_attr(). You should now be able to pass list, tuple, np.ndarray, np.matrix or scipy.sparse, and the elements can be any kind of string, string object, or number. All will be normalized to conform to the spec. 

If the input contains unicode strings, any non-ascii characters are [XML entity encoded](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_XML_and_HTML_character_entity_references). E.g. `25 µl` will be written as the ascii string `25 µl` to the HDF5 file. When read back, any XML entities are unescaped, so that you get the original back. This is all transparent to the Python user: you work with unicode arrays and nevermind how they are stored. At the same time, it ensures interoperability with languages that do not support unicode in HDF5 (such as MATLAB).

You can now directly convert a pandas DataFrame to a row/col dictionary for create(), like so:

```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'col1': [1, 2,3], 'col2': [0.5, 0.75, 1]}, index=['a', 'b','c'])
col_attrs = df.to_dict("list")
```

demonstrate how both positive and negative integers can be mapped to the same bitvector:

    (declare-const i Int)
    (declare-const ibv (_ BitVec 5))
    
    (assert (< i 0))
    (assert (= ((_ int2bv 5) i) ibv))
    (assert (= ibv #b10000))
    
    (check-sat)
    (get-value (i ibv))

    sat

    (declare-const i Int)
    (declare-const ibv (_ BitVec 5))
    
    (assert (> i 0))
    (assert (= ((_ int2bv 5) i) ibv))
    (assert (= ibv #b10000))

Thank you for the elaborate discussion!

> The main objection I see is that a reset_params method would not work on networks with parameter sharing.

Depends on the implementation. If we limit support to initializers returning a numpy array, we can just call `set_value()` on the original shared variable (instead of creating a new one) and all parameter sharing is kept intact. So that negative point can be addressed.

My main objection is that adding a resetting feature that only resets *some* of the weights may lead users into running wrong experiments. But maybe users with custom initializers will become suspicious at how this could possibly work and have a closer look at the documentation.

> "resetable" might even be a tag that can be applied to params to denote if it knows about standard Initializer

Sounds good to me (but I'd prefer `resettable`).

> When dealing with pretrained weights, the user must always explicitly load them and set their value.

The user could call `get_all_param_values` and `set_all_param_values` with `resettable=False` to remember and reset such values if needed.

> Another consequence of implementing this change is that layer parameters don't need to be initialized immediately if the layer knows how to do it.

If implemented, this should be optional. Some existing user code may rely on the weights being initialized exactly when constructing the layer, and changing things will make previous results irreproducible (think of a seeded RNG that will give different results if anything happens in a different order).
In any case, the implementation could use `init.Constant()` for all resettable weights so we create the necessary shared variables, and then rely on `reset_params` to initialize the weights when needed. I think there's no other way to implement this without touching existing layers -- they expect `add_param()` to return a shared variable that is valid for the layer's lifetime.


Now for a name discussion: `reset_params` sounds like it could restore the original state of the network. Maybe `reinitialize_params` would be more suitable. A `reinitializable` tag sounds awkward, though. Is there any third solution?

For the implementation, I'll comment in #740.

[a, b, c] + [d, e] -> [a, b, c, d, e]

> I am attaching a stand-alone script

Thank you for the clear demonstration!

> The key idea is to apply scaling of grid coordinates from [-1,1] to pixel space *before* applying Theta (the affine transform input to the TransformerLayer).

I see.

> The code below is not elegant, and any suggestions on making it simpler would be appreciated.

Well, it seems like the true solution would be defining the grid in pixel space from the beginning. But the question is how to go about the translation in Theta then. If we define this in pixel space as well, it will require values of a much larger magnitude than the scaling parameters, which is potentially bad for optimization. If we leave it in [-1,1] space, the horizontal and vertical translation are defined proportionally to the image size, so for a rectangular image, a translation of [0.1,0.1] will move by different amounts in x and y (that's what currently happens). A solution in between would be a space from -1 to 1 for the larger dimension, and a correspondingly smaller coordinate space for the smaller dimension.

Another aspect is the `downscale` argument. If this is non-proportional, what do we want it to do? Probably we'd also want this to be applied before any rotation in Theta, and not afterwards?

In the long term, it would be good to move this functionality into Theano. There are efficient implementations (e.g., in [Torch](https://github.com/qassemoquab/stnbhwd/tree/master/) or [matconvnet](https://github.com/vlfeat/matconvnet/blob/master/matlab/src/bits/impl/bilinearsampler_gpu.cu)), as well as cuDNN functions which split the implementation into a grid generator (from the transformation matrices) and bilinear sampler (from the grid and input image). These would probably be much faster than what we have now (they just weren't available at the time we added the spatial transformer to Lasagne).

Whatever we decide, we should try to take care that (a) existing networks can be run with future versions of Lasagne and (b) we can use existing implementations to replace the current vanilla Theano implementation in the future.

Would you be interested in bringing this to Theano, or at least have a look into how these existing implementations handle non-square inputs? Do they scale the image after Theta or before?

> we decided it would be best for each tool to be self contained and ship it's own necessary version of sqltoolsservice. This prevents any cross version dependency where if a user has both mssql-cli and mssq-scripter installed, and sqltoolsservice introduces a breaking change, we wouldn't want to break both tools. Rather we want each tool to operate independently with only the latest necessary sqltoolsservice.

I'm not the person to tell you about alternative methods. But I'm fairly certain (99% and some change), that you're going about this wrong and that you'll regret it at some time in the future. I even asked a quick question on [StackOverflow for some clarification,](https://stackoverflow.com/a/48346227/124486)

> To develop with FOO V1 and FOO V2 concurrently on the same computer you would have to use virtualenv and create a separate virtual environment where each version of FOO will reside without interfering. Each will have it's own site-packages carrying each one specific version of the package.

So the python way is to create a different "virtualenv" for each package. It's simply not true that in the  alternative *"each tool [must] operate independently with only the latest necessary sqltoolsservice."* You can upload multiple versions to PyPI and have each tool in their own `virtualenv` tracking what version to use. The advantage being you're not having to maintain each tool for the dependency. You can just operate off of semvar -- a change upstream in `sqltoolsservice` will propagate down when and only when you want.

                         dtype='float', required=False),
        NWBAttributeSpec(name='brain_region_target', doc='Allen Institute acronym',

        NWBAttributeSpec(name='stainings', doc='stainings', dtype='text', required=False),
        NWBAttributeSpec(name='light_source', doc='wavelength of light source in nm',
                         dtype='float', required=False),
        NWBAttributeSpec(name='image_scale', doc='scale of image (pixels/100um)', dtype='float',
                         required=False),

subject = NWBGroupSpec(

                         doc='ID of subject (lab convention)'),

            name='sex', required=False, dtype='text',

        NWBAttributeSpec(name='species', doc='Species of subject', dtype='text',

OpticalFiber = NWBGroupSpec(
    neurodata_type_inc='Device',
    neurodata_type_def='OpticalFiber',
    name='OpticalFiber',
    doc='Meta-data about optical fiber',
    attributes=[
        NWBAttributeSpec(name='type', doc='model', dtype='text', required=False),
        NWBAttributeSpec(name='core_diameter', doc='in um', dtype='float', required=False),
        NWBAttributeSpec(name='outer_diameter', doc='in um', dtype='float', required=False),
        NWBAttributeSpec(name='microdrive', doc='whether a microdrive was used (0: not used, 1: used)',
                         dtype='uint'),
        NWBAttributeSpec(name='microdrive_lead', doc='um/turn', dtype='float', required=False),
        NWBAttributeSpec(name='microdrive_id', doc='id of microdrive', dtype='int', required=False)
    ]
)

ns_builder = NWBNamespaceBuilder(name + ' extensions', name)

specs = (subject, virus_injection, OpticalFiber)
for spec in specs:
    ns_builder.add_spec(ext_source, spec)
ns_builder.export(ns_path)


def obj2docval(spec):

    args_spec = [
                 {'name': 'help', 'type': str, 'doc': 'help', 'default': 'help'},
                 {'name': 'description', 'type': str, 'doc': 'decription', 'default': '?'}]
    for attrib in spec.attributes:
        if attrib.dtype is 'text':
            _type = str
        else:
            _type = attrib.dtype

        arg_spec = {'name': attrib.name, 'type': _type, 'doc': attrib.doc}
        if not attrib.required:
            arg_spec['default'] = None

        args_spec.append(arg_spec)

    for group in spec.groups:
        arg_spec = {'name': group.name, 'type': group.neurodata_type_def, 'doc': group.doc}
        if group.quantity == '?':
            arg_spec['default'] = None

        args_spec.append(arg_spec)

    return tuple(args_spec)


def get_nwbfields(spec):
    vars = [attrib.name for attrib in spec.attributes] + \
           [attrib.name for attrib in spec.groups] + \
           [attrib.name for attrib in spec.datasets]

    return tuple(vars)

####

load_namespaces(ns_path)

@register_class('buz_Subject', name)
class mySubject(original_Subject):

    __nwbfields__ = get_nwbfields(subject)

    @docval(*obj2docval(subject))
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):

        NWBContainer.__init__(self, name='subject', source='source')

        for attr, val in kwargs.items():
            setattr(self, attr, val)

# load custom classes
ns_path = name + '.namespace.yaml'
ext_source = name + '.extensions.yaml'
load_namespaces(ns_path)

subject = mySubject(subject_id='007', genotype='mouse1', species='mouse', sex='U', help='?')

nwbfile = NWBFile("source", "a file with metadata", "NB123A", '2018-06-01T00:00:00', subject=subject)

fname = 'test_ext.nwb'
with NWBHDF5IO(fname, 'w') as io:
    io.write(nwbfile)
```

I think something like quiver could certainly be built with nteract's packages. I say it that way because core to the current nteract desktop app's philosophy is that we work with documents on disk (to saved locations). This also has an impact as the running kernel's current working directory is supposed to be the same directory the notebook is stored in. Now to respond to specifics.

Yes! We should totally do autosave, also posted in #814.

> Every note I make is automatically saved in the "inbox" folder, and I can move it to another folder to categorize. I can also add tags to these notes.

This cross over from hierarchical and into tag based indexing is something I want to explore for a web version of nteract (see https://github.com/nteract/mocks/pull/14/files and https://github.com/nteract/commuter for the current in progress). Whether that means in the documents themselves (which can be properly indexed) or a separate running service, I'm not sure. I would hope that the native OS could support this more easily -- it's pretty clear though that the best UI for this will come from within the app.

> I think the automatic saving and collection will be a welcome addition for a lot of people. As long as you can still save individual notebooks in custom directories, no functionality would be lost.

I like this! It would mean we're performing the indexing yet allowing freedom around the files themselves. Yes! I'd love to see this happen in the long term.

Once you have the notebooks indexed, you can certainly set up the organizational columns, allow for full search (since we're indexing them, might as well).

-----

An aside - on the Mac I use spotlight to find notebooks frequently, though we're missing a Quicklook plugin (#305) as well as any way to help macOS with indexing notebooks properly.

Regarding `travis.yml`, your PR is adding 3 new jobs. I don't think we need to cover that many scenarios, one extra job should be enough. I suggest `travis.yml` should begin with:

```yaml
language: python
python:
  - "3.6"
env:
  - WAGTAIL="wagtail>=2.0,<2.1" DB=sqlite
matrix:
  include:
  # Latest Wagtail version
  - env: WAGTAIL="wagtail>=2.0,<2.1" DB=sqlite
  - env: WAGTAIL="wagtail>=2.0,<2.1" DB=postgres
  - env: WAGTAIL="wagtail>=2.0,<2.1" DB=mysql
  - python: "3.5"
  - python: "3.4"
  # Past Wagtail versions
  - python: "3.6"
    env: WAGTAIL="wagtail>=1.13,<1.14" 
  - python: "2.7"  # Wagtail 1.13 was the latest tested against 2.7
    env: WAGTAIL="wagtail>=1.13,<1.14" 
  - python: "3.6"
    env: WAGTAIL="wagtail>=1.12,<1.13"
    # ...
```
This ensures we test with the latest wagtail version against python 3.6, 3.5 and 3.4 as well as sqlite, postgres and mysql. For older versions we do less tests to keep the travis build snappy.

We should also drop anything below Wagtail 1.8 (penultimate LTS) at some point.

One idea we've bounced around is to support a concretization layer like what is proposed in "In-vivo Symbolic Execution". Basically, past some point on the callstack (i.e., inside the heap functions), any symbolic data they touch is automatically concretized (with the resulting possibility of then executing the functions in unicorn). For stuff like the heap, where you _usually_ aren't expecting symbolic data to be reasoned over, it would provide a very nice speedup, and allow us to more faithfully mirror actual execution.

When angr raises a `SimMemoryAccessError`, it's mostly because the state is not satisfiable.

## Why is the state unsat?

The only way to check if a state is satisfiable or not is to ask the theorem prover (in angr we uses Z3). Sometimes this can be very time consuming. Hence angr provides an option called `LAZY_SOLVES`. With `LAZY_SOLVES` on, whenever a path branches into several at the end of a basic block, angr will not actively check whether each succeeding state is satisfiable or not. Instead, angr assumes they are all satisfiable, and goes ahead to create succeeding paths. If any of those succeeding state is unsat, and later on a solve must occur (like, concretizing a symbolic memory write target), the theorem prover will fail to find a solution to all gathered constraints in that state (since it's unsat), and an exception `SimMemoryAddressError` is raised. In short, `LAZY_SOLVES` is a balance between path explosion (more paths are created) and time spent in constraint solving (we solve less, and hopefully future constraints simply contradict with constraints in the past, which means we don't need to solve for that path at all).

If you disable `LAZY_SOLVES` in the initial state (`p.factory.entry_state(remove_options={simuvex.o.LAZY_SOLVES})`, where `p` is a `Project` object), you won't see that exception.

@zardus Do we want to revise the exception description to make it less confusing?

## Your problem

The problem you are facing is, that a previous input check always fail. How did you "try to avoid this other input check"?

It's likely that there isn't such a path that reaches the verification point without passing the "previous input check". If you make angr avoid that input check, maybe it'll never reach the verification point with a satisfiable state.

Previously next meant "the waypoint you are heading towards". So initially this returns 0, but as soon as you start moving it reports next as "1" the first waypoint. This means that you get next reported as 0 when you're heading towards the first waypoint.

I'm not sure exactly how we missed this since I remember checking 3.2.1 and having it work and then trying 4.0.0 and having it tell me that my Python version (2.6) was too old which made me assume that 4.0.0 was the first to require 2.7.

This library maintains compatibility with Python 2.7 and 3.3+. The `secrets` vs. `random` split in Python 3.6+ is intended to make it easier for people to understand which sources of randomness are cryptographically reliable. Ultimately, `secrets` depends on the system PRNG (via `SystemRandom`, which uses `/dev/urandom`), which is the same interface used by pyotp. If you want to know more about the behavior and security of the system PRNG, please search for something like "is /dev/urandom secure" for an extended discussion.

I will close this PR now, since there is no practical benefit (security or otherwise) from pyotp using the `secrets` module - it already uses the strongest available PRNG via `SystemRandom`.

Some thoughts:
This is a good first step that can help most users. Leaving the management of the ticket lifecycle (ie renewing the ticket) outside of the scope has some advantages: kerberos already comes with an expected set of tools and documentation that can be built upon.

Auto-renewing the ticket ourselves seems like a good optimization (over, say cronjob'ing kinit as was suggested), but there might be some pitfalls: is there enough output for the end-user to troubleshoot? Does it conflict with another renewal mechanism already on the box? Does it properly update the ticket on Windows OS? (I've had issues with a java kerberos lib that required changing registry keys to get permissions to write to the ticket cache folder). Are there scenarios that require turning off auto-renewal and prompt for user creds instead when ticket expires?

On mutual auth: I think it would be good to expose as many dials as requests_kerberos provides in configuration and also adopt its default behavior to avoid surprising the end-user. For instance, if I read their doc correctly, it seems that mutual auth is required by default.

On scenario 5, flowing the ticket to other components (livy, sparkmagic, etc): is this something the component can request from jupyter when needed? Or will it get pushed and cached for later use? In which case the ticket might be stale (not to mention possible security issues). Also, if the purpose is to sign on to external Kerberos servers, we're talking about propagating (and renewing) a different ticket (TGS).

@olegklimov It's still important to respect the action ranges supplied by the envs, even though they may be arbitrary as in the LunarLander case. The fact that LunarLander includes a penalty for actions makes its action range particularly arbitrary/unnecessary; however, simply changing it to (-inf, inf) is problematic because 1) it assumes we have domain knowledge about the environment and 2) you can imagine it could also change the optimal policy. An action space range is simply a constraint of the problem, so the solution can't simply be to change it. Besides, real applications have continuous action spaces that are bounded, so we need algorithms that can deal with them and benchmark environments that can respect them.

A wrapper is a good place to start, though it should be recognized that it's a hack, since as @unixpickle pointed out it will bias the policy gradient. Though I suppose that you had a black-box environment that clipped your actions, you'd never know...

> We don't have a mechanism to "recommend" range to the agent (0..1 vs -1..+1 for example).

This is precisely why implementing a Beta policy is the only non-hack solution that makes sense to me. You can do this "recommending" in a principled way with any distribution whose support is an interval (i.e. no infinity). Simply make `action_space.low` correspond to the lower bound of the support and `action_space.high` correspond to the upper bound. You could also use a Gamma for an environment with an action range of (0, inf), for example.

Should anyone land here wondering about implementation I got around to implementing my own version of the `get_query_fields` function as such:

```
def extract_requested_fields(
    info: graphql.execution.base.ResolveInfo,
    fields: List[Union[Field, FragmentSpread]],
    do_convert_to_snake_case: bool = True,
) -> Dict:
    """Extracts the fields requested in a GraphQL query by processing the AST
    and returns a nested dictionary representing the requested fields.

    Note:
        This function should support arbitrarily nested field structures
        including fragments.

    Example:
        Consider the following query passed to a resolver and running this
        function with the `ResolveInfo` object passed to the resolver.

        >>> query = "query getAuthor{author(authorId: 1){nameFirst, nameLast}}"
        >>> extract_requested_fields(info, info.field_asts, True)
        {'author': {'name_first': None, 'name_last': None}}

    Args:
        info (graphql.execution.base.ResolveInfo): The GraphQL query info passed
            to the resolver function.
        fields (List[Union[Field, FragmentSpread]]): The list of `Field` or
            `FragmentSpread` objects parsed out of the GraphQL query and stored
            in the AST.
        do_convert_to_snake_case (bool): Whether to convert the fields as they
            appear in the GraphQL query (typically in camel-case) back to
            snake-case (which is how they typically appear in ORM classes).

    Returns:
        Dict: The nested dictionary containing all the requested fields.
    """

    result = {}
    for field in fields:

        # Set the `key` as the field name.
        key = field.name.value

        # Convert the key from camel-case to snake-case (if required).
        if do_convert_to_snake_case:
            key = to_snake_case(name=key)

        # Initialize `val` to `None`. Fields without nested-fields under them
        # will have a dictionary value of `None`.
        val = None

        # If the field is of type `Field` then extract the nested fields under
        # the `selection_set` (if defined). These nested fields will be
        # extracted recursively and placed in a dictionary under the field
        # name in the `result` dictionary.
        if isinstance(field, Field):
            if (
                hasattr(field, "selection_set") and
                field.selection_set is not None
            ):
                # Extract field names out of the field selections.
                val = extract_requested_fields(
                    info=info,
                    fields=field.selection_set.selections,
                )
            result[key] = val
        # If the field is of type `FragmentSpread` then retrieve the fragment
        # from `info.fragments` and recursively extract the nested fields but
        # as we don't want the name of the fragment appearing in the result
        # dictionary (since it does not match anything in the ORM classes) the
        # result will simply be result of the extraction.
        elif isinstance(field, FragmentSpread):
            # Retrieve referened fragment.
            fragment = info.fragments[field.name.value]
            # Extract field names out of the fragment selections.
            val = extract_requested_fields(
                info=info,
                fields=fragment.selection_set.selections,
            )
            result = val

    return result
```

which parses the AST into a `dict` preserving the structure of the query and (hopefully) matching the structure of the ORM.

Running the `info` object of a query like:

```
query getAuthor{
  author(authorId: 1) {
    nameFirst,
    nameLast
  }
}
```

produces

```
{'author': {'name_first': None, 'name_last': None}}
```

while a more complex query like this:

```
query getAuthor{
  author(nameFirst: "Brandon") {
    ...authorFields
    books {
      ...bookFields
    }
  }
}

fragment authorFields on TypeAuthor {
  nameFirst,
  nameLast
}

fragment bookFields on TypeBook {
  title,
  year
}
```

produces:

```
{'author': {'books': {'title': None, 'year': None},
  'name_first': None,
  'name_last': None}}
```

Now these dictionaries can be used to define what is a field on the primary-table (`Author` in this case) as they'll have a value of `None` such as `name_first` or a field on a relationship of that primary-table such as field `title` on the `books` relationship.

A simplistic approach to auto-applying those fields can take the form of the following function:

```
def apply_requested_fields(
    info: graphql.execution.base.ResolveInfo,
    query: sqlalchemy.orm.Query,
    orm_class: Type[OrmBaseMixin]
) -> sqlalchemy.orm.Query:
    """Updates the SQLAlchemy Query object by limiting the loaded fields of the
    table and its relationship to the ones explicitly requested in the GraphQL
    query.

    Note:
        This function is fairly simplistic in that it assumes that (1) the
        SQLAlchemy query only selects a single ORM class/table and that (2)
        relationship fields are only one level deep, i.e., that requestd fields
        are either table fields or fields of the table relationship, e.g., it
        does not support fields of relationship relationships.

    Args:
        info (graphql.execution.base.ResolveInfo): The GraphQL query info passed
            to the resolver function.
        query (sqlalchemy.orm.Query): The SQLAlchemy Query object to be updated.
        orm_class (Type[OrmBaseMixin]): The ORM class of the selected table.

    Returns:
        sqlalchemy.orm.Query: The updated SQLAlchemy Query object.
    """

    # Extract the fields requested in the GraphQL query.
    fields = extract_requested_fields(
        info=info,
        fields=info.field_asts,
        do_convert_to_snake_case=True,
    )

    # We assume that the top level of the `fields` dictionary only contains a
    # single key referring to the GraphQL resource being resolved.
    tl_key = list(fields.keys())[0]
    # We assume that any keys that have a value of `None` (as opposed to
    # dictionaries) are fields of the primary table.
    table_fields = [
        key for key, val in fields[tl_key].items()
        if val is None
    ]

    # We assume that any keys that have a value being a dictionary are
    # relationship attributes on the primary table with the keys in the
    # dictionary being fields on that relationship. Thus we create a list of
    # `[relatioship_name, relationship_fields]` lists to be used in the
    # `joinedload` definitions.
    relationship_fieldsets = [
        [key, val.keys()]
        for key, val in fields[tl_key].items()
        if isinstance(val, dict)
    ]

    # Assemble a list of `joinedload` definitions on the defined relationship
    # attribute name and the requested fields on that relationship.
    options_joinedloads = []
    for relationship_fieldset in relationship_fieldsets:
        relationship = relationship_fieldset[0]
        rel_fields = relationship_fieldset[1]
        options_joinedloads.append(
            sqlalchemy.orm.joinedload(
                getattr(orm_class, relationship)
            ).load_only(*rel_fields)
        )

    # Update the SQLAlchemy query by limiting the loaded fields on the primary
    # table as well as by including the `joinedload` definitions.
    query = query.options(
        sqlalchemy.orm.load_only(*table_fields),
        *options_joinedloads
    )

    return query
```

@michaelsembwever re: `start_trace`
>What this method ultimately does is create a span (with or without context to a pre-existing trace/span).

I think it does 2 things: decide whether to start/continue a trace, then create eithe r a tracer span or a no-op span.  `start_trace` isn't ideal, but it has more of a role than just span creation, otherwise we'd just use a `Span` constructor.

@yurishkuro @michaelsembwever re: 3-cases
> I'm arguing that they are in fact not.

I'm somewhere between the two positions: merge `start_trace` and `join_trace`, leave in-process span creation separate.  The reasoning is as follows.

Tracers benefit from information about whether they are at a process boundary or not (I've sometimes referred to these as service entry points above, not sure what is clearest). This information, denoted by an API method used for such entry points, would be used by some population of tracers to think about sampling or grouping of data. Therefore the in-process child span seems to be in a separate class.

I'm still in favor of merging `start_trace` and `join_trace` methods that might be used at entry points, however, because leaving them separate delegates unnecessary context management logic to the instrumentor, logic that would be better reserved for tracer implementers.  This is noted by @michaelsembwever above and @yurishkuro in another thread.

I can see some benefits of combining `start_trace` and `join_trace` in the old API since their usage is mostly boilerplate, purely dependent on the availability of `trace_context` decoded from the request.

But in the new API without `trace_context` such merge is more problematic, because the decoding of the context is no longer separate from starting a new span.

So while this is a good PR for the old interface, I would rather not merge it, since it will make the existing libraries that already make use of Python API incompatible.

So the condition to throw an exception would vary per computer per writing task. To be honest, I do not know how to find the universal boundary condition.

I found a couple of threads relevant for this top in stackoverflow:

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/21328884/openpyxl-writing-large-excel-files-with-python
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/16608028/set-cell-format-and-style-using-optimized-writer-in-openpyxl

I've been trying to think about how to handle the shell builtins, and I'm not entirely sure. I noticed while looking around that it seems most of the really commonly-used Bash builtins happen to also have executables in the path macOS and Ubuntu (that are presumably shadowed by the shell implementation). But still a few other potentially useful commands like "wait" seem to be built in to the shell (not that you would want to tab-complete wait for a file anyways...)

Also, shell built-ins that do not have corresponding executables are often be tied to the state of the shell itself, which cmd2 doesn't seem to maintain (right?). For example jobs, fg, bg can't really be used.

It seems like there's a limited number of concrete options on how to handle this:

1. Use `type` on macOS/Linux and investigate a Windows solution to cover all the bases.
2. Create a set of common builtins that should be tab-completable and check if the command is in the path *or* this set.
3. Ignore the shell builtins because it should be "good enough" since there are overlapping executables in the path.
(then of course things like abandoning default_to_shell completion altogether)

I think 1 would be a bit too hacky and could blow up if everything was attempted to be covered (I think using shell=True uses the shell from the `SHELL` environment variable, so if they had a shell that doesn't support the `type` command... well...

Actually, the above statement made me realize, that trying to detect shell built-ins would pretty much be an undecidable problem since we presumably have no idea what shell is going parse the commands.

I think I'll stop typing this essay because I'm not sure exactly where I'm going with this. TL;DR I think it becomes a headache when thinking about builtins and that the consequences of ignoring them would be minimal.

Thanks for getting back to me (and for your work on these modules!)

I kind of like all of the proposed solutions. Because I'm a bit behind schedule I'll submit a PR tomorrow for a single URL for Mandrill. That should help people with a similar configuration (described below) over to anymail from djrill.

Between solution 2 and 3 I think I'd go with 2. It will be more performant as signatures only need to be calculated a single time regardless of success. A loop over multiple keys will have the wrong key much of the time depending on how many expired keys are in the list and the order they're in.

The configuration of solution 2 is also more clear. It you have both inbound and tracking webhooks in Mandrill and you need to change one of them it's going to be easier to review.

If solution 2 defaults back to `MANDRILL_WEBHOOK_KEY` when `MANDRILL_INBOUND_WEBHOOK_KEY` or `MANDRILL_TRACKING_WEBHOOK_KEY` aren't present then it means you can more clearly maintain configurations where a single webhook is being used.

Solution 3 has the benefit of being more intuitive and less verbose. I looked into the documentation and codebase to see if it was supported while debugging. If other ESPs support multiple keys for rotation it makes a lot of sense and the performance of the module may not really be a big issue.

Unfortunately, Mandrill handles webhook keys in a clunky way. There is only a single key and it's generated by Mandrill. Mandrill begins sending requests signed with the new key immediately and will retry the requests every few minutes until a 200 response is received (IE, the app is updated). With a large volume and/or a slow deployment process it could lead to a lot of repeated requests that fail.

What do you think about having all three solutions implemented?

---

Regarding our configuration:

We were using the `DJRILL_WEBHOOK_URL`. Right now I'm defining `MANDRILL_X` and the `ANYMAIL['MANDRILL_X']` in the following way:
```
MANDRILL_WEBHOOK_URL = '<full url>'
ANYMAIL = {
    'MANDRILL_WEBHOOK_URL': MANDRILL_WEBHOOK_URL,
    ...
}
```

We have had some issues with mandrill in the past and use the mandrill module and these settings directly in a few other spots. Once I get this working I'll be cleaning up the settings where I can.

---

And finally, so that you have a bit more information regarding Mandrill configuration...it's possible to create a webhook in Mandrill in two ways:
1. Creating an inbound routing rule which lets you set the recipient part of the email address for a domain. Tracking can be added to this webhook later.
2. Creating a webhook via Settings > Webhooks. It has tracking options but I don't see a way to add inbound routing to an existing webhook.

For our domains we created the inbound webhook and then add tracking options to it so we end up with a single key and a single webhook URL.

Ok, digging a bit more -- this *is* the expected behavior (which could be documented a bit more I suppose).  My explanation at the beginning was bogus.

When you hand pybind11 an object that is known already, it *will* give you back the corresponding PyObject wrapper *no matter what the return value policy is*.

Return value policies specify what is done when pybind11 doesn't know the object yet -- in that case, the lifetime and ownership of the value needs to be clarified.

If you want to ensure that a copy is made, you'll have to modify your code as follows:

```
A a = b; 
return py::cast(a);
```

A return value policy doesn't need to be specified.

This could be solved by letting it destruct throughout the whole program, but that felt somewhat dirty.

What the program does is just scraping websites for specific entries as worker threads. When the program is happy with the amount of entries it no longer want anything to do with the threads. Seems simple on paper, but in practice it turns out that's not the case (at least when involving Python).

Nevertheless, this fixes the last issue in the mentioned program. Hopefully I wont stumble upon some lurking fatal errors later on. In that case I'll see about going over message-based termination again.

Despite different certs using the same installer class, you still need to call restart once for each cert to make sure the appropriate remote web server is restarted. So technically the reason for this is that Certbot doesn't know that the subsequent request for the certificate with both names will still fail, so it attempts it again, because *the requested certificate is different from the existing certificate*. (Since the requested certificate is different from the existing certificate, the expiry time isn't checked at all!)

That is, when you have a certificate for `test1.example.org` (only), and then you run `certbot -d test1.example.org -d test2.example.org`, with or without `--allow-subset-of-names`, Certbot says "OK, I have a certificate for only one of those names, I was asked to get a certificate for both, I'll try to do that".

And then it fails, but because of the `--allow-subset-of-names`, the renewal is still performed.

In particular, we don't have logic for "the subset of names that succeeded is a match for the existing certificate which is not near expiry". It might be better for rate limits if we did introduce something like that.

In defense of the current behavior, I would say "please don't run Certbot with `-d` options unless you actively, specifically want it to make a renewed effort to get coverage for those names". (Note that `certbot renew` would *not* show the same behavior here, because it would take the list of desired names from the existing certificate.) However, maybe we could be cleverer about rate limits in this particular situation.

Right now the "should we try to renew/issue" logic happens very early in the process. Once the renewal attempt is triggered, we never second-guess that decision and say "oh, I guess we actually shouldn't have tried to issue this certificate after all". Instead, we just look at whether the issuance succeeded or failed, and save the new certificate if the issuance succeeded. In this case it seems like you're looking for a later decision like "OK, we tried to obtain a new certificate as requested by the user *but it turned out in practice* that the specific new certificate that we would get wouldn't be particularly useful, so we don't actually want to issue it".

We could try to implement logic of that sort, but I'd point out that you can probably still hit the failed authorization rate limit (rather than the duplicate certificate issuance limit) by rerunning the command, because Certbot will never stop trying to get the authorizations for the `test2.example.org` name (it never knows ahead of time that that attempt is going to fail just because it failed before). So you would never get to a situation where repeatedly running `certonly --allow-subset-of-names` with specific `-d` options for previously-failed validations doesn't result in an attempt to get new authorizations or thereby eventually run into a rate limit. Admittedly, it could be a gentler rate limit than the one that is currently hit.

@jsha @bmw I don't think those issues are common. They're typically due to "personal firewall" software, probably not on Linux or other typical Certbot platforms, and probably not on servers.

Since we haven't found any other options (Other that `Alias` inside `Location`, which only works in recent Apache 2.4 releases) I do think we should seriously consider that `ProxyPass` option. As far as we know, it will work in all cases.

Some reasons I was nervous about it:
* it's more complicated than a pure Apache change
* we'd need to tweak the standalone plugin to make sure it listens only on localhost for this use (or on a UNIX domain socket?)
* we'd need to find a reliably free port
* we'd need to make sure we never have the problem of ports being unavailable for 30 seconds after they're closed

To be clear I think all of this is totally solvable, but might be a source of bugs or complexity if we do this in a hurry.

Ray requires a large shared-memory file system (on Linux it is often half the size of the total memory). In the absence of that it probably won't be possible to return large objects from remote functions or to pass large objects into remote functions.

As for the serialization issue, can you try `pip install -U ray`? We just pushed 0.2.2 to PyPI and that should improve the pandas integration.

Where do you want to store the dataframes (if not in the object store)? You could of course write them to disk. I'm not sure what you mean by "global cache variable that I maintain".

I'm not sure about the exact change between 0.3.1 and 0.4.0, but the fundamental issue is the following:

When a remote function is defined, its definition is exported to the workers (assuming `ray.init()` has been called). If `ray.init()` has not been called, then the definition will be exported when `ray.init()` is called.

When a remote function is invoked (e.g., `f.remote()`), then we don't ship the function definition, we just ship the function ID, then the worker uses that ID to lookup the appropriate function and execute it.

When you call `ray.worker.cleanup()`, we're killing all of the workers and other Ray processes. We also reset some state on the driver.

Then when you call `ray.init()` again, the remote function does not get exported again. Then when you call `f.remote()` the driver or worker or some process looks for the remote function with the given ID, but it can't be found because some state was flushed when we did `ray.worker.cleanup()` and was never recreated.

You are doing it wrong... This is the correct way:

```bash
streamlink --player "open" --player-args " -W -n -a '/Applications/QuickTime Player.app/Contents/MacOS/QuickTime Player' '{filename}'" --player-passthrough hls URL QUALITY
```

If you don't understand the syntax, then you should read Streamlink's `--player-args` parameter documentation. `{filename}` is the variable that will be replaced with the stream URL when preparing the player parameter line. In the man pages of `open`, you can see that you can also use shorthand names for registered apps. This was working on my system, so I shortened the string, hoping to make it a bit more clear.

What I have posted is working 100%. As I've already explained, the issue with the player arguments I've posted first was that Quicktime does only accept file names as first parameter. Again, if you don't understand the difference between these two command posted by me, read the `open` manual (`man open`).

Before such a player preset you're requesting here can be added to the Twitch GUI, a couple of things need to be changed, because this is using some workarounds. But that's not the right place to discuss here, because it's not the Twitch GUI repo.

I'm closing this thread here now, since the question has been answered and a command line which can be used to watch streams via Streamlink and Quicktime on macOS has been provided.

It seems that this is driver's SELECT issue.

From ClickHouse [docs](https://clickhouse.yandex/docs/en/single/#datetime):
> Date with time. Stored in four bytes as a Unix timestamp (unsigned).

>The date with time is converted from text (divided into component parts) to binary and back, using the system's time zone at the time the client or server starts. In text format, information about daylight savings is lost.

> By default, the client switches to the timezone of the server when it connects. You can change this behavior by enabling the client command-line option --use_client_time_zone.

On INSERT datetime converts into timestamp. On SELECT timestamp converted into datetime with UTC timezone now. DateTime should be [constructed](https://github.com/mymarilyn/clickhouse-driver/blob/0.0.9/src/columns/datetimecolumn.py#L16) using server or client timezone, not UTC.

You can switch to UTC timezone as temporary solution.

No worries! If you're coming from other libraries then Trio really requires a shift in perspective. I think we've all been there :-).

> That said, while this solves my particular use-case, it still feels odd to me that there is no task object that I can use to cancel, join (and potentially retrieve the return value) of an individual task. I don't currently have a need for it, but I strongly feel that without it, something is lacking in the API.

So it's funny: I felt that way too! The first release of Trio had all that stuff. ([Check it out.](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/v0.1.0/reference-core.html#getting-results-from-child-tasks)) But what I found over ~a year of working with thing was that... basically what you just said kept happening over and over. "Well, it turns out in *this* case it's actually simpler not to use that stuff... but surely *next time* we'll want it!" Except we never did. And it turned out to create awkwardness for really useful changes, like `nursery.start` [1] and [simplified nurseries](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/136), which turned out to critical for all kinds of things (e.g. absolutely needed for pytest-trio, trio-asyncio, websocket libraries, ...). So in 0.2.0 it got deprecated, and 0.3.0 removed it. This also removed a ton of API complexity: looking at the [0.2.0 release notes](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/history.html#trio-0-2-0-2017-12-06) I count **14** classes/methods/etc. that were removed or moved out of the top-level `trio` namespace because of this change.

It's possible that the problem with the old API was in the details, not the concept; maybe another version of the same basic idea would be more useful. But I'm fine with deferring this until someone comes up with a proposal for what a better version would look like, with more compelling use cases. And I believe you can implement such an API as a library on top of what Trio has now, so there's nothing stopping folks from experimenting with new APIs here if they want to. So I'm going to leave this closed for now.

Thanks for bringing this up though – it's always useful to review 

[1]  If `start_soon` returns a `Task`, that's easy to ignore when you don't want it, but if `start` returns a value + a `Task`, then you end up writing `(thing_i_care_about, _) = await nursery.start(...)` all the time. Cf. `sock.accept()`, which I *always* get wrong because I forget that it returns two values, one of which I never care about.

Sure, that would work. There's a sample (untested) future implementation [here](https://gist.github.com/njsmith/aa269020e40cc137936805d5d5bbdd91), that takes advantage of trio's `Result` abstraction to simplify things a bit compared to the curio one.

You could also drop down a layer of abstraction fairly easily, using Trio's `wait_task_rescheduled` primitive. This is how things like `Event` are implemented internally:

```python
@attr.s
class Connection:
    # maps request id -> waiting task
    _outstanding_requests = attr.ib(default=attr.Factory(dict))

    # Let's assume someone calls nursery.start_soon on this when the connection is opened
    async def response_task(self):
        async for response in ...:
            waiting_task = self._outstanding_requests.get(response["id"])
            if waiting_task is None:  # must have been cancelled XXX
                continue
            if "value" in response:
                result = trio.hazmat.Value(response["value"])
            else:
                result = trio.hazmat.Error(RequestFailedError(response["error"]))
            trio.hazmat.reschedule(waiting_task, result)

    async def send_request(self, ...):
        id = self._get_next_id()
        await self._send_the_actual_request_on_the_wire(id, ...)
        # Register ourselves to be woken up with the response when it arrives
        this_task = trio.current_task()
        self._outstanding_requests[id] = this_task
        # If we get cancelled, unregister ourselves and carry on (XXX discussed below XXX)
        def abort_fn(_):
            del self._outstanding_requests[id]
            return trio.hazmat.Abort.SUCCEEDED
        # Go to sleep until response_task wakes us, or we're cancelled,
        # returning/raising whatever value/error it gives us
        return await trio.hazmat.wait_task_rescheduled(abort_fn)

I don't have a strong feeling about which way is better. This one uses slightly trickier interfaces, so might be more error prone. But we've dropped a whole layer of abstraction, and the end result isn't really any more complicated -- it might actually be fewer lines of code than the Promise based approach? -- so maybe it's simpler overall. And one thing this lower-level interface does do is remind you to think about cancellation.
</details>

Cancellation is an important and interesting issue for this problem, specifically: what do you want to happen if `conn.send_request` gets cancelled? Does our protocol have some way to propagate a cancellation across the wire? If so, do we want to wait for the cancel to be acknowledged? My solution above assumes (specifically in the lines marked XXX) that in response to cancellation we want to just abandon our outstanding request to continue on without us, and ignore whatever response comes back... but this is definitely not the only plausible semantics :-).

There's a more fundamental objection to special casing "await foo()": it breaks the notion of "in the absence of scope changes, any subexpression can be replaced with a name referring to that subexpression without altering the semantics of the overall expression"

That is:

    await foo()

would no longer mean the same thing as:

    extracted = foo()
    await extracted

and that's simply not OK, even you come up with some clever implementation hacks to enable them to be different.
also see https://confluence.lsstcorp.org/display/DM/Data+Butler+Working+Group

Yes, for compatibility with v1.0 the default values were left as is so that anyone who has used FAIR in the past doesn't get any surprises when they try to run the latest version. r0=35 gives an ensemble median CO2 in 2017 of 403 ppm, i.e. consistent with observations (of course, the influence of the recent El Nino has affected this, and FAIR only responds to forced variability, but we're probably within 1-2ppm of the long-term trend). Addition of the non-CO2 forcing and use of a different TCR/ECS prior to Millar et al will have changed the results slightly relative to that paper. CO2 concentration wasn't a formal constraint on the ensemble but could (arguably should) be used as such. I can't remember what the median CO2 concentration was for r0=32.4, but I'm sure it wasn't much different to 403.

Thank you, that helps me understand what's happening.

The way I ended up fixing the problem last night was similar ( though I
didn't understand why this fixed the problem ):

?atom_expr: "new" funccall -> newfunccall
          | funccall
          ...

funccall: atom_expr "(" [arguments] ")"

In dealing with this sort of problem in my own latest parser, I drew a
distinction between manually created aliases and automatically generated
ones. Auto-generated aliases always get merged into the parent rule ( so
they end user never receives them in the AST ). You could apply similar
logic here. If you're auto-splitting a rule, flag the duplicate as such and
prevent it from triggering the assert.

A couple counter-points to consider:

1) some users might intentionally want multiple rules to combine into the
same alias because it makes their post-processing logic work better. They
therefore definitely wouldn't want that assert firing or want a mechanism
to tell the engine to allow it in specific circumstances.

An example of where this might occur is a tool that's converting data from
one format to another. Think in terms of converting a proprietary document
to xml. It's not unreasonable to imagine they might want to generate the
same xml tag from multiple rules/aliases in the parse tree. Yes, I know
this example is a bit contrived and that the combining logic might be
easier done in the post-processing logic instead of the grammar, but I'm
trying to look at it from both perspectives and there could be some
practical benefits to this approach.

2) I'm finding I'm moving this logic in a direction where I'm splitting
rules into separate aliases to simplify the post-processing logic. For
example, I have split classdef into 2 separate rules, one with subclassing
and one without. With this change, I get a predictable number of children
of each alias.

Here's what I've done with classdef to illustrate #2:

classdef: "class" NMAE  "(" arguments ")"  ":" suite -> subclassdef
        | "class" NAME ["("           ")"] ":" suite

now, in the implementation for "classdef" I can do something like this:

name, suite = classdef.children
create_class ( name, [], suite )

and for "subclassdef" I can do:

name, bases, suite = subclassdef.children
create_class ( name, bases.to_list(), suite )

I'll probably rewrite funccall to the same effect:

funccall: atom_expr "(" ")"
        | atom_expr "(" arguments ")" -> funccallwithargs

So, in conclusion:

I don't think the assert should go away, but it should give a more
actionable error message, but be suppressed if the duplicate was created by
the engine's auto-refactoring ( because that will only confuse people -
especially newbs to your library like me ).

If it's not too difficult, it might not be a bad idea of having a way to
suppress the alert for specific aliases.

A global flag to disable the assert all-together might be useful in
specific circumstances, but it's use should be discouraged.
